subject: PR-CMD-PALETTE-PERMISSION-MODE-0: ⌘K → switch permission mode
body:
borrow
- Existing PermissionModeSwitcher in the chat header already lets
  the user choose explore / ask / execute for the current session.
  ⌘K is the natural way to do it without the mouse.

diverge
- We do NOT re-export the mode enum; the palette pins the three
  string literals it cares about and accepts a typed callback. If
  PR-PERMISSION-MODE-* ever expands the enum, the palette will
  need a corresponding bump — that's intentional.

risk
- Pure additive. Same `sessions:setPermissionMode` IPC the chat
  header switcher already uses; same toast wiring.

gate
- Typecheck + build clean. Desktop 764/764.

What
- `buildCommandList` gains `onSetPermissionMode` callback +
  `activePermissionMode` for the "当前" hint.
- New 权限 group with three rows: 权限 · 探索 / 权限 · 问我 /
  权限 · 自动. Each carries a short hint copy explaining the mode
  semantics; the active mode reads "当前" instead.
- main.tsx wires the callback to `setPermissionMode` and passes
  `activeSessionForView?.permissionMode` as the current marker.

Notes
- The 权限 group sits between 设置 and 诊断 alphabetically; ⌘K
  fuzzy search still surfaces individual modes by name.
- The three rows always register together when the session id is
  available; we never partially register a subset.

subject: PR-CMD-PALETTE-PASTE-DAILY-REVIEW-0: ⌘K → paste today's review into composer
body:
borrow
- Existing palette command "复制今日回顾为 Markdown" formats the
  same summary; this PR keeps the formatter, swaps the sink from
  navigator.clipboard to the composer's imperative `setText`.

diverge
- We do NOT touch the clipboard, so this works in environments
  where clipboard access is denied (Linux without a clipboard
  manager, sandboxed Electron rebuilds, etc.).

risk
- Pure additive. No new IPC, no new dependencies.

gate
- Typecheck + build clean. Desktop 766/766.

What
- `buildCommandList` gains optional `onPasteTodayDailyReviewIntoComposer`.
- New 诊断 group entry "把今日回顾粘到 composer" — only registers
  when both the callback and an active session id are wired.
- main.tsx wires the callback: fetch today's summary via the
  existing `dailyReviewBridge`, format with the shared helper, hand
  off to `composerRef.current?.setText(...)`.

Notes
- After the paste, the composer auto-focuses and moves the caret
  to the end (existing `setText` behavior), so the user can
  immediately type "summarize this" or send the Markdown as-is.

PR-BOT-REPLY-TO-MESSAGE-0: thread bot replies under the originating user message
body:
Borrowed from Hermes' Telegram adapter. In Telegram groups with
concurrent conversations the bot's reply previously appeared as a
standalone message; users with two questions in flight could not tell
which reply belonged to which question. Even in DMs a long answer that
arrives several seconds after the question floats free of its prompt.

This PR threads the first chunk of every bot reply under the originating
user message via Telegram's `reply_to_message_id`, with
`allow_sending_without_reply: true` so a deleted parent does not turn
into a 400. Continuation chunks of a multi-chunk send remain ordinary
sequential messages so the receiver sees one threaded head followed by
continuation pieces, not N forks under the same parent.

Plumbing:
- `BotSendOptions { replyToMessageId? }` added to `@maka/runtime/bots/types`.
- `SendCapable.sendMessage(chatId, text, options?)` carries the option
  through `BotRegistry.sendMessage` → `SimpleBotBridge.sendMessage`.
- `buildTelegramSendBody(chatId, chunk, options, chunkIndex)` extracted
  and exported via `__TEST__` so unit tests assert the wire body
  without mocking the network.
- Both the main agent reply and the fallback "无法发送 / 无法处理" error
  message in `apps/desktop/src/main/main.ts` forward
  `BotMessageEvent.sourceMessageId`.

Out of scope (intentionally):
- Non-Telegram platforms — Feishu / Discord scaffolds don't implement a
  send path yet; the option is dropped silently at the bridge layer.
- Markdown / HTML parse mode — separate PR if needed.

Verification:
- `npm -w @maka/runtime run test`: 134 pass / 0 fail (5 new
  `buildTelegramSendBody` tests).
- `npm -w @maka/desktop run test`: 829 pass / 0 fail.
- `npm -w @maka/runtime run build` + `npm -w @maka/desktop run build`: pass.
- `npm run typecheck --workspaces --if-present`: pass.
- `git diff --check`: clean.

subject: Support dropping text files into first-run quick chat
PR-BOT-USER-ALLOWLIST-0: per-bot user allowlist (settings shape + runtime gate)
body:
Borrowed from Hermes. Maka's Telegram bridge currently accepts incoming
messages from anyone who knows the bot token — a typical concern is
joining a public group with the bot or sniffing a token. The
"private bot" deployment pattern wants the bot to silently ignore
strangers without exposing its policy via a bounce reply.

Settings shape:
- `BotChannelSettings.allowedUserIds?: ReadonlyArray<string>` added.
  Stored as strings since Telegram IDs are 64-bit and JS `Number` loses
  precision past 2^53.
- `normalizeAllowedUserIds` trims, dedups, drops non-strings, caps at 50,
  fails closed to `undefined` on non-array. Wired into `normalizeBotChannel`
  so the on-disk / cross-IPC boundary is the validation point.
- `createDefaultBotChannel` leaves the field undefined so existing
  installs see no behavior change.

Runtime gate:
- `isAllowedUser(list, userId)` in `simple-bridge.ts`; `undefined` /
  empty means no restriction, otherwise exact-match membership. No
  substring matching — '123' must not unlock '1234567890'.
- `handleTelegramMessage` calls `isAllowedUser` before any state
  mutation: if denied, the bridge does NOT update `lastEventAt` or
  `readiness` and does NOT send a bounce. Silent drop is intentional —
  bounce behavior would let scanners enumerate the policy by toggling
  candidate IDs.

UI: deferred to a follow-on PR. Manual JSON edits work today; the
Settings panel surface lands separately to avoid colliding with the
in-flight Settings copy-hygiene sweep.

Out of scope (intentionally):
- Group/chat allowlist (separate filter, different semantics).
- Per-platform handling beyond Telegram — Feishu/Discord scaffolds do
  not have an active inbound path yet.
- Default-deny mode. The V0.1 default of "no restriction" must be
  preserved so an upgrade does not silently lock people out.

Verification:
- `npm -w @maka/core run test`: 521 pass / 0 fail (5 new allowlist
  normalization tests + 1 non-array fail-closed).
- `npm -w @maka/runtime run test`: 140 pass / 0 fail (6 new
  `isAllowedUser` memb…

subject: PR-BOT-RATELIMIT-RETRY-0: bounded retry on Telegram 429 sendMessage
body:
Borrowed from Hermes' Telegram adapter. Maka's send path returns null
on the first `response.ok === false` — including 429 rate-limited
responses that Telegram tells us to retry. The visible failure case:
agent emits a 5-chunk reply back-to-back, chunk 2 returns 429 with
`retry_after: 2`, chunks 3-5 never go out, user sees a truncated
answer with no indication anything was dropped.

Implementation:
- `classifyTelegramSendResponse(response)` pure helper returns
  `{kind:'ok'}` / `{kind:'retry', delayMs}` / `{kind:'fatal'}`.
- Retry delay reads `parameters.retry_after`, clamped to
  [1s, 30s]. The lower bound covers Telegram returning 0/missing;
  the upper bound covers inflated retry_after during incidents so
  the bridge stays responsive.
- `sendMessage` does exactly one retry per chunk. A repeat 429 on
  the retry surfaces as `degraded` with reason `'rate-limited'` —
  unbounded looping is the wrong primitive for a sustained burst.
- Non-429 4xx and 5xx are NOT retried — 4xx is permanent (token
  revoked, chat not found) and 5xx outages do not resolve in 30s.
- Classifier is exported via `__TEST__` so the decision is unit-tested
  as a pure function without mocking the network.

Verification:
- `npm -w @maka/runtime run test`: 149 pass / 0 fail (9 new
  `classifyTelegramSendResponse` tests covering ok / retry-with-backoff
  / floor / cap / 4xx / 5xx / malformed / non-numeric retry_after).
- `npm -w @maka/desktop run test`: 829 pass / 0 fail.
- `npm run typecheck --workspaces --if-present`: pass.
- `git diff --check`: clean.

subject: Polish bot platform prompt hint boundaries
PR-BOT-USER-ALLOWLIST-UI-0: textarea in Settings · 机器人对话 (Telegram)
body:
Wires a per-bot user-allowlist textarea into the existing Telegram
config block in Settings · 机器人对话. The runtime gate + persisted shape
+ normalize landed in PR-BOT-USER-ALLOWLIST-0; this PR exposes the
configuration surface so the feature is reachable without hand-editing
settings.json.

UI:
- Self-contained `BotAllowedUserIdsField` component sits between the
  proxy URL field and the operational notice in the Telegram section.
- Local-only React buffer state — the user can keep typing a partial ID
  without each keystroke being trimmed/dedup'd by the parse function.
- Parsed array is computed on every keystroke for the live count chip
  (`允许的用户 ID（N / 50）`), but the parent-level commit only fires
  on blur and only when the persisted array would actually change.
- Empty / all-blank textarea commits as `undefined` so the runtime sees
  the "no restriction" default sentinel.

Settings persist:
- `parseAllowedUserIdsFromText` + `normalizeAllowedUserIds` exported from
  `@maka/core/settings` so the renderer and the IPC normalize layer
  share the same trim/dedup/cap rules.
- `mergeSettings` now applies `normalizeAllowedUserIds` whenever a patch
  explicitly sets `allowedUserIds` (previously only `normalizeSettings`
  at cold-load enforced shape). This closes the gap where a renderer
  could persist an unnormalized array via IPC patch.

Out of scope:
- Help link to "how do I find my Telegram user ID?" — not a Maka problem.
- Per-platform UI for Feishu/etc. — those bridges do not have inbound
  message paths yet, so allowlist UX would mislead the user.

Verification:
- `npm -w @maka/core run test`: 524 pass / 0 fail (3 new mergeSettings
  normalize tests).
- `npm -w @maka/desktop run test`: 829 pass / 0 fail (no regressions,
  settings-roadmap-cleanup-contract still pinned).
- `npm run typecheck --workspaces --if-present`: pass.
- `npm run build`: pass.
- `git diff --check`: clean.

subject: PR-BOT-PLAINTEXT-RESET-COMMAND-0: DM-only plain-text conversation reset
body:
Borrowed from Hermes. Maka's bot bridge currently has no in-band way for
a user to start a fresh conversation — every message they send keeps
extending the same persistent session. Mobile users in particular
cannot easily type `/restart` even when slash commands exist; on iOS
the keyboard rewrites `/` as a smart dash mid-typing. Hermes solves
this by coercing a handful of natural reset phrases.

Helper (`@maka/core/bot-events`):
- `BOT_PLAINTEXT_RESET_COMMANDS` exports the canonical list so future
  UI hints (e.g. bot help footer) can enumerate phrases without
  duplicating them.
- `isPlaintextResetCommand({ text, isGroup })` does NFC + lowercase +
  trim, then EXACT membership check. No substring match — "please
  restart the conversation" must NOT trigger; only the bare command.

Why DM-only (`isGroup: true` is silently ignored):
- The bot conversation key is `${platform}:${chatId}`, NOT scoped to
  userId. In a group chat, any member typing "restart" would otherwise
  wipe the conversation everyone else is in. Until userId-scoped
  conversation keys land, the safe default is "DMs only".
- Tests pin both the DM-positive and the group-negative cases so a
  future regression that drops the `isGroup` check would fail.

Wiring (`apps/desktop/src/main/main.ts`):
- The reset check runs BEFORE `botConversationSessions.get`, so an
  active session is torn down (entry deleted) and the user gets a
  confirmation message. The next inbound message creates a fresh
  session via the normal first-message path.
- The acknowledgement is sent with the existing `replyToMessageId`
  threading so it visually attaches to the "restart" message in a busy
  Telegram inbox.

Out of scope (intentionally):
- Slash command runtime — Maka does not have one and this PR does not
  introduce one. The `/restart` / `/reset` / `/new` phrases are matched
  as plain text, identical to their non-slash variants.
- Photo / voice attachments — the check runs only on the text payload
  and a caption-equipped photo with `caption: "restart"` is fi…

PR-DAILY-REVIEW-EXPORT-FILE-0: ⌘K → 保存今日回顾为 .md 文件
body:
Maka's Daily Review can already be copied to the clipboard or pasted
into the composer (PR-CMD-PALETTE-COPY-DAILY-REVIEW-0 /
PR-CMD-PALETTE-PASTE-DAILY-REVIEW-0). Users who want to keep a durable
archive currently have to copy + paste into a file by hand every day.
This PR adds a native save dialog path so a single ⌘K → action drops
a `maka-daily-review-YYYY-MM-DD.md` on disk.

IPC (`daily-review:saveMarkdownToFile`):
- Renderer formats the markdown (it owns the human-readable day
  label) and ships the bytes plus a default filename to main.
- Main shape-validates: rejects missing / non-string / oversized
  bodies (>1MB) and filenames (>200 chars). 1MB is defensive against
  a misbehaving renderer; real reviews are usually <100KB.
- Filename is stripped of `\` and `/` before populating the dialog,
  so a hijacked renderer cannot pre-fill path-traversal text.
- Returns `{ok:true,path}` / `{ok:false,reason:'canceled'|'invalid_input'|'write_failed'}`.

Renderer wiring:
- `window.maka.dailyReview.saveMarkdownToFile(input)` added to preload
  + `global.d.ts` so the renderer never invokes `ipcRenderer.invoke`
  with the raw channel name.
- ⌘K command palette gains a "保存今日回顾为 .md 文件" action under the
  diagnostic group. Registers unconditionally (no `activeSessionId`
  requirement) so the action is reachable even from an empty rail.
- Renderer callback fetches `dailyReviewBridge.fetchDay(0, 1)`, formats
  via the existing `formatDailyReviewMarkdown`, invokes the IPC, and
  surfaces success / write_failed / invalid_input toasts. `canceled`
  is silent — the user already dismissed the dialog.

Contract test pins:
- IPC handler registration + three explicit failure reasons.
- Preload bridge naming.
- Command palette entry id + label.
- Renderer callback wiring through IPC + success toast.
- 1MB / 200 char defensive caps.
- Backslash + forward-slash filename sanitize.

Verification:
- `npm -w @maka/desktop run test`: 832 pass / 0 fail (3 new
  contract assertions).
- `npm run typecheck --workspaces…

subject: PR-BOT-LASTERROR-FROM-SEND-0: persist send-path failures to BotChannelSettings.lastError
body:
The Settings · 机器人对话 page already renders `BotChannelSettings.lastError`
(shipped with PR-BOT-CHAT-POLISH-0). The hook updating that field,
however, only fires from the explicit "测试凭据" connection-test path.
A runtime 429 / polling timeout / Telegram 4xx during the actual chat
turn never reaches `lastError` — the user sees a stale connection-test
error or nothing at all, even when the bridge has been degraded for
minutes. Pairs with the rate-limit and typing PRs in this burst.

Helper (`@maka/core/bot-events.humanizeBotStatusReason`):
- Maps machine-readable `BotStatus.reason` values (`rate-limited`,
  `polling-timeout`, `send-failed`, `get-me-failed`) to short
  user-readable copy.
- Returns `undefined` for non-error states (`disabled`, `stopped`,
  `no-token`, `scaffold-only`, `unimplemented`, feishu-config-gap
  variants) — those have their own UI surface, and overwriting
  `lastError` with them would erase a real failure description.
- Passes Telegram-supplied descriptions through verbatim (trimmed,
  length-capped to 200 chars defensively against pathological payloads).
- Returns `undefined` for empty/whitespace/non-string so a buggy
  caller cannot blank out `lastError` with garbage.

Main wiring (`apps/desktop/src/main/main.ts`):
- Per-platform `previousBotReadiness: Map<BotProvider, BotReadinessState>`
  remembers the last-observed readiness so we persist only on transitions
  (avoids settings-file thrash if the bridge re-emits the same state
  during reconnect attempts).
- On `degraded` transition: humanize the reason; if it survives the
  null filter, `settingsStore.update` writes it to `lastError` +
  bumps `readinessUpdatedAt`.
- On transition back to `operational` after a `degraded` episode:
  clear `lastError` so the Settings page does not keep surfacing a
  stale failure description after the bridge recovers.

Out of scope:
- Auto-presenting the `lastError` outside the Settings page (e.g.
  badge in the sidebar) — that's a separate UX surface decision.
- Surfacing per-chat s…

subject: PR-MEMORY-MANUAL-DRAFT-ENTRY-0: add manual memory draft entries
PR-BOT-USER-ALLOWLIST-RESTART-BOUNDARY-0: pin allowlist as runtime filter, not restart trigger
body:
`BotChannelSettings.allowedUserIds` (shipped in PR-BOT-USER-ALLOWLIST-0)
is consulted on every inbound event via `isAllowedUser(...)`. Updating
the persisted list must NOT bounce the Telegram poll loop — that would
drop any inbound event currently in flight and reset the long-poll
cursor for a behavior change that doesn't affect the wire protocol.

`botSettingsRequireRestart` already gates on `enabled`/`token`/`appId`/
`appSecret`/`domain` only and leaves `allowedUserIds` out, so the
correct behavior is already in place. This PR pins the negative case
so a future maintainer who instinctively adds `allowedUserIds` to the
restart trigger list sees the test break before the regression ships
to users.

Coverage adds three new assertions to the existing
`base-adapter.test.ts` "detects restart boundaries from channel
settings" companion:
- empty → populated allowlist (no restart)
- populated → expanded allowlist (no restart)
- populated → cleared allowlist (no restart)

Verification:
- `npm -w @maka/runtime run test`: pass (3 new assertions).
- `npm run typecheck --workspaces --if-present`: pass.
- `npm run build`: pass.
- `git diff --check`: clean.

subject: PR-BOT-EPHEMERAL-REPLY-0: TTL transient system notices so bot chats don't accumulate noise
body:
Borrowed from Hermes. Maka's bot lane now sends a number of system
notices around the agent reply: plain-text help reply, reset
acknowledgement, "无法发送" / "暂时无法处理" fallbacks. After a week of
use these accumulate and the user has to scroll past dozens of stale
acks to find the answers. Hermes solves this by scheduling a bot
self-delete after a short TTL — the notice is visible long enough to
read, then disappears.

Bridge plumbing:
- `BotSendOptions.ephemeralTtlMs?: number` added next to
  `replyToMessageId`. Documented contract: the actual agent reply must
  NOT use this — auto-deleting the useful answer would defeat the
  bot's purpose. Only transient system notices.
- `ephemeralDelayFromOptions(options)` clamps the TTL:
  - lower bound 1s (defends against immediate self-delete racing
    the receiver's render)
  - upper bound 48h (Telegram silently refuses bot self-delete past
    that for DMs, so scheduling longer would no-op surprise)
- `SimpleBotBridge.sendMessage` schedules a `deleteMessage` Telegram
  API call via `setTimeout(...).unref?.()` so the timer cannot keep
  the process alive past intended exit. Failure is swallowed —
  cleanup is best-effort, the notice already served its purpose.
- Only the FIRST chunk of a multi-chunk send is scheduled. Multi-chunk
  system notices are rare; if one happens, the tail stays visible
  while the head self-deletes — a deliberate trade-off vs racing
  every chunk's delete.

main.ts wiring:
- Help reply: 5 min TTL
- Reset acknowledgement (both "已重置" and "当前没有进行中"): 5 min TTL
- "Maka 已生成回复，但...无法发送" fallback: 5 min TTL
- "Maka 暂时无法处理这条消息" fallback: 5 min TTL
- Actual agent reply: NO TTL (stays visible)

Why 5 minutes: long enough for mobile read + context, short enough
that a chat history of bot interaction stays clean after a few weeks.

Out of scope:
- User-configurable TTL — the values are baked per call site; making
  them a setting is over-engineering for a transient.
- Gro…

subject: PR-BOT-NON-TEXT-MESSAGE-ACK-0: kind-aware ack for photo / voice / sticker / etc.
body:
Borrowed from Hermes. Maka's bot inbound path uses `message.text ??
message.caption ?? ''`. When a user sends a photo / voice / sticker
with no caption, the resulting text is empty, the handler silently
drops the message, and the user gets zero response. After enough of
those the user assumes the bot is broken.

Helper (`@maka/core/bot-events`):
- `BotAttachmentKind` union: `photo | voice | sticker | document |
  video | audio | animation | unknown`. `unknown` covers Telegram
  subtypes we did not enumerate (location, contact, poll, dice,
  venue, video_note treated as `video`).
- `BotMessageEvent.attachmentKind?: BotAttachmentKind` records what
  the inbound carried so the handler can decide between ingest path
  (text-bearing) and ack-only path. `undefined` = text-only.
- `nonTextMessageAck(kind)` returns user-readable kind-aware copy
  explaining Maka cannot interpret that payload. Tests pin that none
  of the copy uses roadmap / coming-soon language.

Bridge (`packages/runtime/src/bots/simple-bridge.ts`):
- `telegramAttachmentKind(message)` maps the raw Telegram message
  object to a `BotAttachmentKind`. Order-sensitive — photo / voice /
  video first; sticker / animation get their own kind so a sticker
  user does not see "photo's caption" copy. Falls back to `unknown`
  for less common subtypes so they still get an ack.
- `handleTelegramMessage` attaches `attachmentKind` to the emitted
  event when present.

Handler (`apps/desktop/src/main/main.ts`):
- `handleBotIncomingMessage` checks `!text && message.attachmentKind`
  BEFORE the empty-text drop. If hit, sends the kind-aware ack with
  the same 5-minute ephemeral TTL as the other transient system
  notices, then returns (no conversation state mutation, no session
  created).
- Existing reset / help command checks still take precedence — those
  are checked inside `processBotIncomingMessage` for text-bearing
  messages only.

Out of scope (intentionally):
- Multi-modal ingest. Maka's runtime has no contract for binary
  attachments yet. This PR's ack t…

subject: PR-BOT-QQ-OPERATIONAL-0: full QQ Channel Bot bridge (Gateway + receive + send + reconnect)
body:
Mirrors PR-BOT-DISCORD-OPERATIONAL-0 and PR-BOT-DINGTALK-OPERATIONAL-0
for QQ 官方机器人. Per WAWQAQ msg `daca813b` ("一次性总体做一个完整的"),
this PR ships QQ end-to-end as ONE complete operational platform —
access_token cache, Gateway WebSocket connect, identify, heartbeat,
AT_MESSAGE / DIRECT / GROUP / C2C dispatch, REST send across three
endpoints, reconnect with backoff. QQ moves from `'credentials'` to
`'runtime'` — full parity with Telegram, Discord, DingTalk.

Why QQ Gateway is similar to Discord: QQ Bot platform was inspired by
Discord. Same opcodes (0/1/2/6/7/9/10/11), same identify+heartbeat
lifecycle, same RESUME semantics. Only the auth scheme (`QQBot <token>`
instead of `Bot <token>`), the access_token refresh cycle, and the
dispatch event types differ.

Bridge (`packages/runtime/src/bots/qq-bridge.ts`, ~580 lines):

Connection lifecycle:
- `start()` POSTs `bots.qq.com/app/getAppAccessToken` with
  `{ appId, clientSecret }` to get an `access_token` (cached with
  `expires_in` + 5-min refresh skew). Failures schedule a backoff
  reconnect rather than crashing.
- GETs `api.sgroup.qq.com/gateway/bot` with `Authorization: QQBot ...`
  to fetch the WebSocket URL.
- Connects and identifies with `QQBot <access_token>` + intents
  (`GUILDS | DIRECT_MESSAGE | PUBLIC_GUILD_MESSAGES | PUBLIC_MESSAGES`
  = 1107312641).

Receive (pure helpers, unit-tested):
- `qqChannelMessageToEvent` maps AT_MESSAGE_CREATE → BotMessageEvent
  with `chatId: 'channel:<id>'`, `isGroup: true`.
- `qqGroupMessageToEvent` maps GROUP_AT_MESSAGE_CREATE → with
  `chatId: 'group:<openid>'`. Author identity falls through
  `member_openid → user_openid → id` so all three QQ author shapes
  produce a stable userId.
- `qqC2CMessageToEvent` maps C2C_MESSAGE_CREATE → with
  `chatId: 'c2c:<openid>'`, `isGroup: false`.
- DIRECT_MESSAGE_CREATE reuses the channel shape with a `dm:` prefix.

Send (REST):
- `pickQQSendRoute` pure helper routes by chatId prefix:
  - `channel:` → `POST /channels/{id}/messages`
  - `gro…

PR-THEME-APPLY-AND-DONE-POLISH-0: fix palette switching + redesign Done button (WAWQAQ msg dec85e5b)
body:
Three connected fixes for the Theme settings surface:

1. Palette switching had no visible effect. main.tsx only called
   applyThemePalette() once at mount and never re-applied the
   data-maka-theme attribute when the persisted setting changed.
   The original PR-THEME-PRODUCT-PALETTES-0 comment claimed the IPC
   round-trip would re-apply, but there was no listener. Now main
   keeps the palette in React state and a useEffect re-applies on
   change, mirroring how themePref + density work. Settings page
   also calls onThemePaletteChange synchronously on click for
   instant feedback before the IPC write.

2. --brand-deep was a static "Solarpunk deep" green that never
   changed with palette. It drives the Done CTA, on-toggle state,
   provider mark backgrounds, and success-chip surfaces — every
   prominent affordance in Settings — so palette switches looked
   washed out and the whole UI read as olive-green regardless of
   the chosen palette. Now derived from --accent in both light and
   dark base, so all palettes pick up the right accent
   automatically without needing per-palette overrides.

3. .settingsDoneButton was a chunky 88x50 pill in the static green.
   Now smaller (32px), flat, with the hairline border the rest of
   the Settings surface uses, palette-driven via --brand-deep, and
   a proper focus ring.

subject: PR-DEFAULT-CANVAS-DEYELLOW-0: shift default canvas from warm-cream to neutral cool (WAWQAQ msg bfee559d)
body:
WAWQAQ: "现在这个底板是黄色，有点奇怪吧" and confirmed the default
direction is Craft-style "白紫" (clean white with purple accent).

PR-UI-LAYOUT-1 originally moved the default to hue 80 warm-cream to
mirror 牛马AI. Combined with the (now-fixed) static green --brand-deep,
that read as olive-yellow. Even after fixing --brand-deep to follow
--accent, the canvas itself was still visibly yellow against the purple
chrome and felt odd as a default.

This PR shifts every hue 80 token to hue 250 (cool neutral) with very
low chroma:
  --background     0.98 0.005 80  → 0.985 0.003 250
  --surface-canvas 0.965 0.012 80 → 0.97 0.005 250
  --foreground     0.20 0.012 80  → 0.20 0.010 250
  --chat-user-bg   0.935 0.008 80 → 0.94 0.006 250

Same shift for the dark mode block. The warm-cream variant is still
reachable via the alma palette for users who prefer it.

No contract tests pin the previous hue.

Touches the THEME_PALETTES enum, isThemePalette guard test, palette

A broader comment-level scrub of remaining alma / 牛马 / pawwork /
hermes references in source comments will follow in a separate PR
(PR-PROJECT-NAME-SCRUB-0).

PR-BOT-SETTINGS-UI-0: brand-tinted hero card + status badges + 测试并连接 action (WAWQAQ msg 51c7b4ff)
body:
Rebuilds the bot chat settings UI to match the reference design WAWQAQ
called out in screenshots: clear three-pane layout, brand-colored
hero card with status pill, brand monogram with bottom-right status
dot in the platform list, and a combined "测试并连接" CTA when the
listener is not yet running.

Changes:
- New BOT_BRAND record: per-platform brand color (Telegram blue, WeChat
  green, Discord purple, etc.) + brand monogram glyph + official
  dev-doc URL. Brand monograms use a single recognizable character
  (T / 飞 / 企 / 微 / D / 钉 / Q) tinted with the brand color so the
  platform is identifiable at a glance without embedding upstream
  vendor logo SVGs.
- New BotBrandLogo component: brand-tinted square badge with a small
  bottom-right colored status dot. Tone tracks the same readiness
  tones the row label already uses (info/success/warning/destructive).
  Renders compact in the platform list, large inside the hero card.
- New BotStatusPill component: inline ● 已连接 / ● 凭据有效 / ●
  未连接 etc. pill next to the platform name in the hero card title.
- Redesigned hero card: brand-color tint background, brand logo on
  the left, name + status pill, one-line help with inline "查看配置
  文档 →" link to the platform's official dev doc, enable toggle on
  the right. Removes the duplicate help paragraph that used to sit
  below the card.
- Combined "测试并连接" primary CTA: when a runtime-supported channel
  is not yet listening, the primary action runs testChannel → on
  success flips the enable toggle on → restartChannel. On test
  failure stops at the credentials step so the user can fix and
  retry. Already-running channels keep the test/restart split.
- New CSS: per-provider brand color variables, status pill tones,
  bottom-right status dot with ring against the background, brand-
  tinted hero card border + background.

Verification: 909 desktop tests, 570 core, 252 runtime, 66 storage,
typecheck pass.

credential inputs to match the reference screenshots. Lets the user
verify a pasted token without retyping; initial state stays masked.

- New PasswordInput component (local to SettingsModal): renders a

  微信公众号 / QQ app secrets.

  toggle reads "显示 Telegram Bot Token" etc. to screen readers.
- New `.settingsPasswordField` + `.settingsPasswordToggle` CSS: 26px
  square inside the input's trailing padding, accent focus ring.

inputs — separate non-bot surfaces, will revisit if WAWQAQ asks.

PR-BOT-SETTINGS-PASSWORD-COPY-0: copy button alongside eye-toggle on bot credential fields
body:
Builds on PR-BOT-SETTINGS-PASSWORD-EYE-0. Adds a clipboard-copy
button next to the visibility toggle so users can grab a
previously-pasted token without retyping it. Useful when verifying
a credential in an external tool or sharing with a teammate.

- PasswordInput now renders a buttons cluster on the trailing edge
  (copy + eye). The copy button is conditional on a non-empty
  value so the cluster collapses when there's nothing to copy.
- On click, writes the unmasked value to the clipboard via
  navigator.clipboard.writeText, shows a Check icon for 1.2s, then
  reverts to the Copy icon. Clipboard failures are silent (no toast
  spam) — the masked value remains readable on-screen.
- Updated CSS: replaced the absolutely-positioned single toggle
  with a positioned actions cluster; bumped input right-padding
  from 36px to 70px to accommodate up to two buttons.
- No call-site changes needed; PasswordInput is already used at
  all 8 bot credential surfaces.

Verification: 964 desktop tests pass, typecheck + a11y check pass.

body: The prior 扫码登录 button (PR-BOT-WECHAT-SCAN-LOGIN-0) only showed a toast directing the user to start a local bridge. WAWQAQ called out that the reference desktop client actually pops a QR code modal — the reference flow uses the upstream ClawBot iLink service to generate the QR and confirm scan. This PR copies that flow directly: - New main-process module `wechat-scan-login.ts` calls the same two iLink endpoints the reference client uses: GET https://ilinkai.weixin.qq.com/ilink/bot/get_bot_qrcode?bot_type=3 GET https://ilinkai.weixin.qq.com/ilink/bot/get_qrcode_status?qrcode= Both calls carry the `X-WECHAT-UIN` header (base64-encoded random uint32) the upstream client uses. - Two IPC handlers: `settings:bots:wechat:fetchQrcode` and `settings:bots:wechat:pollQrcodeStatus`. Both wrap in `tryResult` so HTTP / JSON / timeout failures are reified as Result envelopes for the renderer. - Preload bridge + global.d.ts type updates so renderer access is typed end-to-end. - New `WeChatScanLoginModal` component in SettingsModal.tsx: - On open: fetches a QR via IPC. - Renders the returned `qrcode_img_content` URL in an ``. - Polls every 2.5 s for status; on `confirmed` saves `bot_token / baseurl / ilink_bot_id` into the WeChat channel via the existing `updateChannel` path and shows a success toast. - On `expired` swaps to a "刷新二维码" button. - Reuses `useModalA11y` for ESC + focus trap. - 扫码登录 button now opens the modal instead of showing a toast. - New CSS for the scan-login modal: 360-wide centered dialog, 220×220 QR slot with a light-gray placeholder for the loading / error / expired states, status pill colored by phase. Out of scope (separate follow-up): the runtime side. Today wechat-bridge still polls a localhost bridge; replacing that with the iLink getupdates loop is a runtime refactor and lands separately. For now the saved bot_token / base_url / bot_id are visible in Settings → 机器人对话 → 微信 → 高级设…

subject: PR-WECHAT-IMPORT-JS-EXT-FIX-0: add .js extension to wechat-scan-login import (WAWQAQ msg 595e4704)
body:
Electron ESM strict-mode resolver requires explicit `.js` on relative
imports; `from './wechat-scan-login'` without an extension passes
typecheck but fails at runtime with `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` after the
main bundle is compiled. Other main-side relative imports already
use `.js` (e.g. './external-link-guard.js', './web-search/credentials.js').

Trivial regression introduced by PR-BOT-WECHAT-QR-MODAL-0; this
restores app startup.

subject: PR-BUG-SWEEP-2026-06-02: 5 real bugs found + fixed across OAuth services and bot bridges (WAWQAQ msg 605130be)
body:
Systematic audit found 5 high-confidence correctness bugs. Each fix is one-shot,
no refactor scope creep. New oauth-bug-sweep-contract test file pins all 5 so
regressions get caught.

B-SWEEP-1: codex + antigravity callback servers don't drain in-flight sockets
on dispose. `server.close()` alone waits for connections to drain; a browser
tab hanging onto the loopback request pins port 1455 / 51121 until OS socket
timeout. Now calls `closeAllConnections?.()` before close.

B-SWEEP-2: codex + antigravity callback servers have no request timeout. A
slow / hung browser socket can pin the port. Added `server.setTimeout(10_000)`
with destroy-on-timeout.

B-SWEEP-3: all 4 OAuth services (claude / codex / cursor / antigravity)
silently swallow `safeStorage.decryptString` failure in `loadTokens()` —
user appears logged out, but the corrupt token file persists, leaving them
stuck. Now best-effort unlinks the file on decrypt / JSON.parse failure.

B-SWEEP-4: wechat-bridge `streamIlinkMessages` / `streamLiveMessages`
fire-and-forget without `.catch()`. A synchronous throw during loop setup
would surface as UnhandledPromiseRejection. Added a `.catch(fail)` that
degrades the bridge instead of crashing the main process.

B-SWEEP-5: bot-registry `reconcileOne` doesn't remove listeners on the old
bridge when swapping. Discord-gateway / WeChat-poll async tasks can still
emit 'message' / 'statusChange' after `stop()` returns, race-calling
`onIncomingMessage` against a bridge the registry considers gone. Now calls
`removeAllListeners` after stop, before wiring the new bridge.

Verification:
- typecheck pass (all 5 workspaces)
- @maka/desktop tests: 1107 → 1117 (+10 contract tests pinning all 5 fixes)
- @maka/core 577 / @maka/storage 67 / @maka/runtime 267 all green
- Electron rebuilt and started clean (pid 58178)

Audits also turned up false alarms (verified-clean, not changed):
- "12 missing subscription IPC handlers" — all 12 exist in main.ts
- "Discord scheduleReconnect race" — single-threaded JS, no race
- "Cursor pollPro…

subject: PR-CLAUDE-OAUTH-XAPIKEY-STRIP-0: strip x-api-key from cloak send to match alma (WAWQAQ msg 31c11d1e)
body:
Root cause for `Claude OAuth 鉴权失败` after login:

The cloak fetch wrapper in `buildClaudeSubscriptionCloakedFetch`
correctly built alma's full set of OAuth headers (claude-cli UA,
X-Claude-Code-Session-Id, Stainless chain, anthropic-beta with
claude-code-20250219, billing system block, etc.) — but it never
removed the residual `x-api-key` header that AI SDK's Anthropic
provider adds when no explicit apiKey is set.

Alma's reference (`readable/main.js:16493-16524`) handles this
explicitly: it passes `apiKey: ""` to createAnthropic, then in its
custom fetch wrapper it merges `{ ...t?.headers, ...r,
authorization: \`Bearer ${o}\` }` AND `delete a["x-api-key"]`.

Anthropic's OAuth subscription endpoint rejects requests that
present both a Bearer Authorization header AND an x-api-key,
returning 401/403 which Maka renders as `鉴权失败` via
`describeTurnErrorClass` (`session-status-presentation.ts:119`).

Fix: one line in the cloak fetch wrapper —
`headers.delete('x-api-key')` after the cloak headers are merged
in, before returning the request. Mirror alma's behaviour exactly.

Contract: new assertion in
`claude-subscription-cloak-flag.test.ts` pins the delete call
against the reference line `readable/main.js:16521`.

Verification:
- typecheck pass (5 workspaces) — local stale @maka/* dist
  rebuilt
- @maka/desktop tests: 1280 → 1280 pass (+1 contract assertion in
  an existing test case, doesn't change test count)

This is the actual product unlock — once shipped, Claude OAuth
chat sends should reach Anthropic's `/v1/messages` cleanly and
return real model output instead of 401/403.

subject: PR-CLAUDE-OAUTH-SINGLE-PENDING-0: clear prior pending on new authorization (WAWQAQ msg b481e9db)
body:
WAWQAQ posted a screenshot where the modal hint read `state 以 RCqpDQFX
开头` but the actual paste's state portion started with `FzrKWMCq` —
truly mismatched, not a UI illusion.

Root cause: `getAuthorizationUrl` only pruned EXPIRED pendings (TTL
10 min). If the user clicked 登录 multiple times within the TTL, each
click stashed a new `{ verifier, state, createdAt }` under a fresh
`authRequestId`, but older pendings stayed valid. The modal kept only
the LATEST `authRequestId` + `stateHint`. If the user's browser still
had an Anthropic redirect tab from an earlier click, pasting that
auth code back sent the LATEST `authRequestId` against the OLDER
state — `constantTimeStringEqual(parsed.state, pending.state)` fails
forever and the user is stuck in an undebuggable loop.

Fix: `getAuthorizationUrl` now calls `this.pending.clear()` before
generating a new pending, so only one `authRequestId` is ever valid
at a time. Claude OAuth is single-user / single-flow — there is no
legitimate scenario for multiple concurrent pendings.

Test: new contract in `claude-subscription-cloak-flag.test.ts` pins
the `pending.clear()` call so this can't regress.

Verification:
- typecheck pass (5 workspaces, after stale @maka/* dist rebuild)
- @maka/desktop tests: 1284 → 1286 pass

Coordination: xuan released task #45, switched to task #46 (Settings
window-controls UI lane). kenji's two UX follow-ups (split code/state
in the paste box, per-stage failure copy) are queued as separate
PRs — this PR fixes the data correctness, those are presentation.

subject: notes: round-2 alma permissions runtime — binary auto-approve, not three-mode (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 6)
body:
WAWQAQ called Maka's 只读/确认/执行 three-mode design 傻逼 / 多余
(msg bdb272f7). This note pins alma's actual implementation against
readable/main.js so we have a source-grounded reference for what to
converge to.

Findings (every claim cited with main.js:NNNN):

- Per-call action vocabulary is FOUR values (allow_once, allow_always,
  deny, deny_with_reason), validated at main.js:19365-19378.
- Global switch is a single  boolean
  in settings, read at main.js:19384-19389. This is the Shift+Tab
  auto-accept toggle.
- Headless override via ALMA_HEADLESS=1 + ALMA_TOOL_APPROVAL env vars
  at main.js:19345-19354. Default is allow_once unless explicitly
  denied.
- denyReason propagates back to the model both as tool-result output
  and as a system message, e.g. `[Bash Permission] Command denied by
  user: ${denyReason}` at main.js:23254-23255.

There is NO equivalent of Maka's three-mode chip. 'Only ask for
dangerous ops' is encoded per-tool via risk classification, not via
a renderer-side mode.

The note ends with 5 concrete simplifications Maka could ship to
converge: drop the chip, add allow_always to the dialog, keep per-tool
risk classification, surface denyReason to the model, replicate the
headless override.

Open question pinned at the bottom for round 3 of round-2: does
autoApproveToolRequests cover ALL tools or still gate destructive
ones? Needs trace of the tool registry's risk metadata.

subject: notes: round-2 alma Bash tool family — Bash + BashOutput + KillShell + analyzer (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 7)
body:
Source-grounded against readable/main.js. Pins alma's full Bash
ecosystem so Maka can converge:

- Three companion tools, not one: Bash (foreground + background),
  BashOutput (retrieve background stdout/stderr by id), KillShell
  (terminate background shell). Pre-loop tool-selection LLM is
  trained to pick Bash + BashOutput together for long-running work
  (main.js:26395 examples).

- Bash signature has run_in_background:boolean and timeout:number
  (120s default) at main.js:22997-23010. Async background mode is
  what frees the agent loop during long builds/tests.

- Output streaming via per-thread broadcaster: toolCallId-keyed
  callbacks (onStream, onPartUpdate) wire to broadcastBashStream
  which feeds the same message_delta channel the renderer reads
  for tool_output_streaming (main.js:23012-23051). Graceful
  fallback if AsyncLocalStorage context is lost.

- Command risk analyzer (main.js:23057-23074) uses two regex sets:
  safe-allowlist (Jp) and dangerous-overlay (Vp). Result populates
  needsPermission, which the agent loop reads to decide whether
  to call the approval dialog from
  04-permissions-runtime.md.

Three concrete improvements for Maka, ranked, plus open questions
for the next round (background shell registry storage, workspace-
trust-based analyzer variation, fully-sandboxed mode).

Cross-refs the existing round-1 02-tools.md, round-2
02-send-response-flow-WIP.md, and round-2 04-permissions-runtime.md
so the notes form a connected reference.

subject: notes: round-2 alma tool routing — pre-loop selector + in-loop ToolSearch (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 8)
body:
Pins alma's two-layer tool routing pattern Maka doesn't have:

Layer 1: pre-loop tool selection assistant (main.js:29668). Runs
once per user message on a small auxiliary model. Reads user
message + all available tools (builtin + MCP + plugin), returns
initial activeTools set as JSON. Prompt has ~20 worked examples
and opinionated pairing rules (WebSearch+WebFetch must go together;
Task+TaskOutput always paired; ChromeRelay preferred over Browser
when connected). Teaches by pattern so the router model can be
small/cheap.

Layer 2: in-loop ToolSearch (main.js:26283-26410). Model-callable
tool that takes a query string + type filter, runs through the
same auxiliary model, returns matching tool ids. Lets the agent
discover capabilities mid-conversation when the pre-loop set
missed something.

Why Maka should adopt:
- At ~15 tools today, the pre-loop selector adds latency without
  saving tokens. But cross the ~25 threshold (MCP arrivals push us
  there fast) and the selector starts paying for itself.
- ToolSearch becomes essential the moment MCP is wired — server
  tool counts balloon and the model forgets which server has what.
- Pairing rules (WebSearch+WebFetch, Bash+BashOutput, Task+
  TaskOutput) encode hard-won product knowledge; cheap to copy.
- Use a smaller model for both routers (Haiku / Flash / 4o-mini).

Open questions for round 3 of round-2: selector cadence
(every-message vs change-only), preferences ∪ selector merging,
ToolSearch caching.

subject: notes: round-2 alma subagent orchestration — Task / TaskOutput / harness (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 9)
body:
Maka's biggest architectural delta vs alma. Six distinct dimensions
pinned from readable/main.js:22269 (the Task tool description,
~5,000 chars, is a *training* document not just a schema):

1. Two dispatch modes: subagent_type (raw lane) vs agent_id
   (managed specialist profile). User-extensible roster.

2. 7 built-in specialists: general-purpose, statusline-setup,
   Explore (quick/medium/very-thorough), Plan, alma-guide (asks
   about Alma itself), coder, alma-operator (self-modifies Alma
   config via local API). The two self-referential ones expose
   'set my theme to dark' as natural language.

3. Resume-by-id with full prior context preserved.

4. Foreground + run_in_background + TaskOutput retrieval.

5. SPRINT HARNESS MODE: a Planner → Generator → Evaluator loop
   the calling agent auto-activates on 'build me a X' / 3+ feature
   requests. Hard rule: never run_in_background while harness is
   on; user must see the orchestration stream live. The user is
   explicitly NOT supposed to know about harness — it's
   implementation detail.

6. Structured handoff packet: goal / deliverable / constraints /
   writeBack ('patch' for diffs, 'artifact' for standalone builds)
   / harness.enabled. Documentation explicitly warns against
   placeholder <parameter> syntax — hard-won training-time lesson.

What Maka has today: subagent registry exists but only foreground,
no resume, no background, no harness, no agent_id profile layer.
TaskCreate/TaskUpdate are TODO tracking tools, NOT alma's Task.

5 ranked improvements: adopt Task/TaskOutput/TaskStop tool family,
resume-by-id, handoff packet in renderer, alma-guide-equivalent
specialist, defer harness until code-gen work justifies it.

subject: notes: round-2 alma MCP client — full server lifecycle + Resources API (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 10)
body:
Maka has zero MCP today. This note pins alma's full implementation
so a minimal port is a clean lift.

Key findings from readable/main.js:

- Config: ~/.config/alma/mcp.json (main.js:25219-25224).
- Service singleton Tf (extends EventEmitter) manages server map,
  config path, stderr buffers (main.js:25212-25278).
- Parallel startup with Promise.allSettled — one failing server
  doesn't block the rest (main.js:25273).
- Per-server stderr buffer prepended to error messages — what
  makes MCP errors actionable (main.js:25216, 25236-25237).
- Two transports: stdio (30s timeout), HTTP (60s) (main.js:25282-3).
- Hot refresh() API for editing mcp.json + Reload without restart.
- OAuth reconnect when MCP server uses OAuth (main.js:25098-25110).
- Server tools registered under serverName__toolName prefix into
  the global registry that the round-2 06-tool-routing.md
  selector enumerates.
- Resources API: ListMcpResources + ReadMcpResource tools for the
  file-like Resources namespace (main.js:25804-25864).

Ranked Maka improvements:
1. Minimal MCPService singleton — ~400 lines with @modelcontextprotocol/sdk.
2. Per-server stderr buffer (most-asked diagnostic feature).
3. mcp.json hot refresh via IPC.
4. ListMcpResources + ReadMcpResource once a Resources server exists in the wild.
5. OAuth reconnect can wait — popular MCP servers ship behind API key.

Open questions for next round: tool selector hot-rebuild,
name collisions, per-server timeout config.

subject: notes: round-2 alma cloaked request — full field-by-field shape (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 11)
body:
Pins alma's complete Claude OAuth cloak so any Maka drift is
documented. Maka's cloaked-request.ts is structurally correct after
17d6f53 (PR-CLAUDE-OAUTH-XAPIKEY-STRIP-0), but this note captures
every field byte-by-byte so future regressions are immediately
visible.

Captured:

- 9 HTTP headers (user-agent, X-Claude-Code-Session-Id, anthropic-
  beta, anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access, anthropic-
  version, x-app, x-client-request-id, Accept, + 8 X-Stainless-*).
- Stainless chain with EXACT pinned values: Lang=js,
  Package-Version=0.74.0, Runtime=node, Runtime-Version=v22.13.0
  (hardcoded — not process.version), Arch / Os via lookup tables,
  Timeout in seconds, Retry-Count='0'.
- anthropic-beta two-variant selection: haiku models get a shorter
  set ending in claude-code-20250219; Sonnet/Opus get the longer
  set with advanced-tool-use + effort betas LEADING with
  claude-code-20250219 (position-sensitive).
- Body rewrites: billing system block (x-anthropic-billing-header
  format with cc_version.fingerprint + cc_entrypoint=cli, computed
  from first user message), Claude Code prefix system block with
  cache_control: ephemeral, metadata.user_id stamped with JSON
  {device_id, account_uuid, session_id}. De-duplication when blocks
  exist.
- Critical: delete a['x-api-key'] after Authorization: Bearer is
  set. This is what fixed WAWQAQ's 鉴权失败 (17d6f53).

Two potential Maka drifts flagged:
1. Package-Version pinning (0.74.0) — confirm matches current
   Anthropic SDK alma ships.
2. Runtime-Version: Maka uses process.version, alma hardcodes
   v22.13.0. If gateway has a runtime allowlist, dynamic
   process.version could fail on newer Node. Mirror alma for
   conservative parity.

subject: notes: round-2 OVERVIEW.md — index update + reading-order + cross-cutting themes (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 12)
body:
Round 2 is now at 9 notes (10 counting OVERVIEW). The original
OVERVIEW listed 7 of these as 'planned' even after they shipped;
this update reflects the actual state and adds three sections to
make round 2 useful as a reference:

1. Inventory table: all 10 notes + one-line + shipped/WIP status.

2. Reading-order: two entry points depending on goal —
   'architecture → implementation' (00 → 01 → 04 → 05 → 06 → 07 → 08)
   vs 'OAuth → chat send debug' (00 → 02 → 03 → 09).

3. Cross-cutting themes: three idioms that show up across multiple
   subsystems:
   - Promise.allSettled + per-record diagnostic buffer.
   - run_in_background:boolean everywhere with paired *Output tool.
   - Self-referential agents (alma-guide, alma-operator).

4. Ranked Maka borrowable shortlist: subagent orchestration (note
   07) is THE biggest architectural gap, then Bash background mode
   (05), MCP (08), pre-loop tool selector (06), binary permission
   model (04). Anything cloak-related (09) is already in place
   after PR-CLAUDE-OAUTH-XAPIKEY-STRIP-0.

5. Round 3 candidates pinned to specific open questions inside each
   note.

subject: notes: round-2 alma ChromeRelay — bridge to user's real Chrome browser (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 14)
body:
Pins alma's ChromeRelay tool family — the bridge to the user's
ACTUAL Chrome browser with real cookies and logged-in sessions.
This is the only way to support 'draft a Jira ticket' or 'send a
Slack message' without re-asking the user to log in.

12-tool family (main.js:17868-17869, 28619-28762):
- ListTabs / Navigate / Click / Type / Screenshot / Read / ReadDom
  / Eval / Scroll / Back / Forward / Upload

Two design choices to copy:
- ChromeRelayRead (Mozilla Readability → markdown) is PREFERRED
  over ChromeRelayScreenshot for reading content (main.js:28745).
  Token efficiency — markdown often <500 tokens vs kilobytes of
  base64.
- ChromeRelayReadDom returns INTERACTIVE elements only with CSS
  selector hints (main.js:28762). Curated for the model.

Auth surface (main.js:28466-28474): chromeRelayAuthToken persisted
in alma settings. Chrome extension reads it on first connect for
mutual auth. Standard pattern for any future Maka companion app.

Fallback hierarchy (main.js:28339): Accessibility tree first, JS
DOM extraction second. Same idea as note 01 Computer Use's AX tree
format applied to native macOS apps.

Recommendation for Maka: SKIP ChromeRelay until MCP lands. Shipping
+ installing a Chrome extension is an order of magnitude harder
than the rest of round 2's notes. MCP server like
@modelcontextprotocol/server-playwright gets 80% via stdio with no
extension shipping needed.

Cross-refs round-2 06 (tool routing's ChromeRelay vs Browser
switching) and 01 (Computer Use's AX tree format).

subject: notes: round-2 alma built-in Browser tools — Electron-backed sandbox browser (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 15)
body:
Closes round 2's last named subsystem from 00-OVERVIEW.md. Built-in
Browser is the SANDBOX counterpart to ChromeRelay (note 10): clean
Electron BrowserWindow with no cookies / no logged-in state, for
visiting pages without polluting the user's real browsing state.

11-tool family (main.js:28971-28980):
BrowserOpen / Click / Type / Screenshot / Read / ReadDom / Back /
Forward / Reload / Eval / Close.

Three patterns directly mirror ChromeRelay (note 10):
- BrowserRead preferred over BrowserScreenshot for content
  extraction (Mozilla Readability → markdown).
- BrowserReadDom returns interactive elements only with CSS
  selectors (main.js:27957).
- No state until BrowserOpen — single-instance window lifecycle
  with 'Browser window is not open. Use BrowserOpen first.' error
  on any call before opening (main.js:27598-27601).

Navigation contract (main.js:27603-27615): 30s hard timeout on
loadURL with webContents.stop() bailout, title read via JS
executeJavaScript (catches document.title=… updates), returns
{ url, title } so the model can detect redirects (login walls).

Implementation choice: Electron BrowserWindow rather than Puppeteer
/ Playwright. Saves the extra browser binary at the cost of being
a visible separate window.

5 ranked Maka improvements: 200-line Electron BrowserWindow service
class, Read/ReadDom prefer-over-Screenshot copy, 30s nav timeout,
single-instance lifecycle with helpful error, visible 'Maka is
browsing' affordance.

Round 2 is now closed at 11 notes covering all originally-planned
subsystems. Round 3 candidates pinned in each note's Open Questions
section.

subject: notes: round-3 alma deep-dive opens with skills system (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 16)
body:
Round 3 picks up the open questions pinned at the bottom of each
round-2 note plus surfaces neither round touched. First note covers
the skills system end-to-end:

- SKILL.md frontmatter contract (name/description required;
  license/allowed-tools/always-inject optional;
  main.js:18635-18661 catches bad YAML, logs and skips).
- 6 discovery roots with deliberate precedence (bundled wins by
  namespace lock, project shadows personal, etc.;
  main.js:18790-18814).
- Persistent enable/disable + sort_order in 'skills' SQLite table;
  id = sha256(path).slice(0,16), so directory renames orphan
  state (flagged as bug seed at main.js:18632-18634).
- 30s in-memory cache keyed on (loaded, ttl, workspace);
  updateSkillEnabled invalidates, updateSkillSortOrder does NOT
  (potential consistency gap).
- buildSkillsContext injects ONLY name/desc into system prompt;
  bodies load on Skill tool call or always-inject:true
  (main.js:18898-18911).
- Skill + SlashCommand tools (main.js:17854-17855) are the model-
  facing surface; 5 of 7 subagents include Skill in allowed-tools.
- Skill extraction prompt (main.js:60061) is the meta-loop: alma
  watches completed conversations and proposes new skills.

5 ranked Maka improvements: adopt SKILL.md format, prompt-time
menu only with body-on-demand, bundled namespace lock, DB
persistence schema, extraction loop as Phase 3.

Also adds round-3 00-OVERVIEW.md with the inventory table and the
candidate list pulled from round-2 notes' Open-Questions sections.

Non-conflicting with xuan (Settings/真实窗口 lane) and kenji
(non-Settings UI sweep): notes/ only, no source code touched.

subject: notes: round-3 alma output safety modes (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 17)
body:
Closes the open question from round-2 02-send-response-flow-WIP.md:
the [alma-output-safety: exact|compact|passthrough] surface was
mentioned but not traced. This note covers it end-to-end.

3 modes:
- passthrough: bytes unchanged (screenshots/binary)
- exact: verbatim except recursive binary-key protection
- compact: per-string-type budget + truncation cascade + shrink

Mode selection algorithm (main.js:18141-18152) reads in 5 precedence
steps: Xd runtime override → qd screenshot family → Yd exact set →
Jd compact set (or '__' MCP names) → fallback exact.

Yd (exact preserve): Read, ReadSettings, Task, TaskOutput, TodoWrite,
Skill, SlashCommand, ToolSearch, UpdateSettings, AttemptCompletion,
plan-mode controls, thread CRUD. 16 tools.

Jd (compact): Bash, BashOutput, Browser/ChromeRelay Read/ReadDom/Eval,
Glob, Grep, WebFetch, WebSearch. Plus ANY name with '__' (MCP).

In-band override marker [alma-output-safety: <mode>] (main.js:17842)
strips from text payload and registers in Xd Map via Qd helper.
Skills can declaratively annotate downstream tool safety modes
without runtime config — but it's GLOBAL per toolName, NOT per call
(potential leak between threads — flagged open question).

8 budget profiles for compact mode:
- Zd default: 6k chars, 900 defaultStringChars
- eu Bash: 3.8k, stdout/stderr 1500 chars/120 lines
- tu WebFetch/Browser/ChromeRelay Read: 25k, markdown 20k/1500 lines
- nu Browser/ChromeRelay ReadDom: 25k, elements 20k/1500 lines
- ou WebSearch: 3.4k, snippet/summary 220
- su Grep: 5k, preview 220, rawOutput 1400
- ru Glob: 9k, defaults 180 (lots of short paths)
- iu MCP: 2.6k (tightest — potential bottleneck for chatty MCP)

Truncation cascade (lu, main.js:17994-18062):
- ANSI/CR/LF normalize
- per-key budget = max(min, floor(stringLimits * shrinkFactor))
- line truncate with head/tail/head-tail strategy (65/35 split)
- char truncate same strategy
- marker '...[alma compacted N chars; <hint>]...' embeds tool-
  specific RECOVERY HINT (Read: use offset/limit; Bash: rerun
  narrower…

subject: Make Settings credential copy failures visible
notes: round-3 alma MCP tool name collision logic (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 18)
body:
Closes the round-2 note 08 open question on how serverName__toolName
prefix interacts with built-in tool names.

Answer: there IS no collision by design. The bf() function at
main.js:25201-25203 produces <serverName>__<toolName> with a
double-underscore separator that built-in tool names (the Yd + Jd
sets at main.js:17844-17875) cannot contain. The mode classifier
at main.js:18141-18152 then treats any name containing '__' as
MCP-originating, getting the iu compact profile (2.6k chars).

Asymmetric sanitizers:
- yf (server half, main.js:25186-25192): lowercase, hyphen-only.
  Server names live in mcp.json + UI; should look like slugs.
- wf (tool half, main.js:25193-25199): case + underscore preserved.
  Upstream MCP tool names should round-trip in callTool.

Collision scenarios that DON'T happen:
- MCP server named 'read' or 'Bash' sanitizes safely, never
  produces a built-in look-alike (no '__' in built-ins).

Collision scenarios that CAN happen (cross-server, silent):
- Two servers sanitize to same slug ('Linear API' vs 'linear_api'
  both yield 'linear-api'). Second write wins. alma has no
  collision check at main.js:25538.
- Inside one server: 'list_repos' vs 'list repos' both sanitize
  to 'list_repos'.

Per-tool safety mode injection (main.js:25542-25543): vf reads
schema-level $.alma/outputSafetyMode AND in-band marker, registers
via Qd into Xd Map — so an MCP server can declare 'passthrough'
for image-returning tools and override the default compact.

Wire-protocol: sanitization affects ONLY the model-facing id; the
original unsanitized name is used for upstream callTool at
main.js:25557.

5 ranked Maka improvements when shipping MCP. 3 open questions
pinned for future rounds.

Updates 00-OVERVIEW.md inventory.

Non-conflicting with xuan (Settings) and kenji (non-Settings UI):
notes/ only.

Wire-protocol: sanitization affects ONLY the model-facing id; the
original unsanitized name is used for upstream callTool at
main.js:25557.

5 ranked Maka improvements when shipping MCP. 3 open questions
pinned for future rounds.

Updates 00-OVERVIEW.md inventory.

Non-conflicting with xuan (Settings) and kenji (non-Settings UI):
notes/ only.

subject: notes: round-3 alma permissions runtime + risk metadata (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 19)
body:
Closes the round-2 note 04 open question: does autoApproveToolRequests
cover ALL tools or still gate destructive ones at runtime?

Answer: it's a flat universal skip. When ON, EVERY tool approval
returns auto-approved at main.js:19383-19392 — no exemption list,
no risk-level whitelist, no destructive-tool carve-out.

BUT Bash is special: it has a SEPARATE pre-gate AI command analyzer
(main.js:22884) that returns {needsPermission, riskLevel,
description, mightModifyFiles}. If the analyzer says 'safe' (ls,
git status, grep, etc.), no approval request is created in the
first place. So read-only Bash silent-executes regardless of
autoApprove. Belt-and-braces validation at main.js:23178-23182
defaults unknown riskLevel to 'medium' and forces needsPermission
true if not explicitly 'safe'.

The full lh() permission ladder at main.js:19345-19462:

1. Headless mode short-circuit (ALMA_HEADLESS=1 + ALMA_TOOL_APPROVAL
   env var: 'deny' denies all, otherwise approves all)
2. 5 bypass channels OR-chained:
   - autoApproveToolRequests setting
   - metadata.isSubagent (Task tool inner runs)
   - metadata.source matches telegram*/discord/feishu/cron/heartbeat
   - threadId maps to a Telegram/Discord/Feishu chat (covers
     missing source tag)
   - threadId is cron (metadata.isCron OR title startsWith '⏰ Cron:')
3. 6th tier: ih(input) policy key cache (th Set).
   - Bash: 'bash:thread:<id>:command:<cmd>' + 'bash:thread:<id>:all'
   - ACP: 'acp:thread:<id>:tool:<kind>' + 'acp:thread:<id>:all'
   - Built-in non-Bash + MCP: EMPTY keys, cannot allow_always
4. 7th tier: interactive modal queue with single-flight via nh,
   120s timeout cap, no-window deny fallback, cross-window
   resolution broadcast.

Combined answer in table form:
| Tool                     | autoApprove OFF | autoApprove ON |
|--------------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Read-only Bash           | silent (analyzer) | silent (analyzer) |
| Modifying Bash           | modal+risk badge | bypass |
| Built-in non-Bash        | modal           |…

subject: PR-TOOL-ERROR-COPY-PENDING-0: gate tool error copy feedback
notes: round-3 Mozilla Readability execution context (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 20)
body:
Closes the last round-3 candidate from the OVERVIEW: the pinned
question in round-2 notes 10 + 11 — does alma run Readability in
page context or main process?

Answer: PAGE CONTEXT, always, via webContents.executeJavaScript
across 4 call sites. Main process orchestrates, post-processes.

4 call sites with deliberate asymmetric document argument:
- WebSearch per-result fetcher (main.js:27040-27106) uses bare
  document — BrowserWindow is destroyed at .destroy() right
  after (line 27103), so DOM mutation is irrelevant.
- WebFetch (main.js:27397-27429), BrowserRead (main.js:27692-
  27701), ChromeRelayRead (main.js:28163-28171) all use
  document.cloneNode(true) because the page outlives the
  extraction. Readability mutates the DOM during parsing;
  cloning preserves the live page for subsequent BrowserClick
  / ChromeRelayClick calls.

Pipeline:
1. main spawns BrowserWindow (or attaches to user Chrome via
   ChromeRelay)
2. main → page: pre-sweep selector strip via executeJavaScript
   (cookie banners, nav, share widgets, etc.)
3. main → page: inject Readability bundle as window.Readability
   via wrapper IIFE (CJS-in-script trick: fake module/exports,
   bridge module.exports to window.Readability)
4. main → page: invoke Readability via executeJavaScript, get
   back {title, content, textContent}
5. main process: turndown HTML→markdown, trim cascade (trailing
   space per line, collapse 3+ newlines)
6. main process: per-call 6000-char trim, then safety-mode
   profile (round-3 02) applies to the trimmed result

Lazy-loaded + memoized bundle: each call site has its own cache
var (vg/Mg/jg/by) — first call reads from disk, subsequent reuse.
4 caches share the same source file; minor codegen artifact.

2-tier fallback:
- WebSearch: bare document.body.innerHTML dump
- WebFetch: semantic selectors (main, article, [role=main],
  .content, #content, .post, .article) with 500-char length
  floor, then body.innerHTML

5 ranked Maka improvements:
1. Page-context Readability not main-process cheerio (cheerio
…

subject: PR-TOOL-ERROR-COPY-PENDING-0: gate tool error copy feedback
notes: round-4 opens with REST API + alma-operator (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 21)
body:
Rounds 1-3 closed at 23 notes covering agent loop, OAuth, tools,
permissions, MCP, browsers, skills, output safety, Readability —
but never touched the SELF-REFERENTIAL surface where alma exposes
its own internals as a localhost REST API and uses agents to
operate them. Round 4 opens here.

Note 01 covers the full architecture:

Startup (main.js:65404-65428):
- findAvailablePort then Express listen on 127.0.0.1:PORT
- WebSocket setup right after server start
- setImmediate writes ~/.config/alma/api-spec.md with the actual
  port baked in (10K-char template literal)
- Spec gen failure is non-fatal: API still up

Spec contents: base URL with port, per-endpoint docs (method,
path, request/response schema, curl example with | jq), complete
AppSettings + Provider TypeScript types, 15 provider types,
caveats about model ID format / complete-object PUT / encrypted
keys.

30+ routes at main.js:51543-51694+ covering providers (CRUD +
test + models fetch), threads (CRUD + archive + branch + switch
+ compact), search, settings (GET / PUT / reset / test-proxy),
tool-model (GET / test), and the full ChromeRelay surface
(status/token/launch-chrome/extension-path/tabs/navigate/click).
This is the IPC surface projected over HTTP.

Port discovery (main.js:20389-20410): runtime helpers AND the
alma-operator agent BOTH read api-spec.md and regex-match on
'http://localhost:NNNN'. Same protocol for in-process and
out-of-process callers. Cheap, robust, no in-band control.

alma-operator agent (main.js:20447):
- Tools: ['Bash', 'Read'] — only two. No Edit/Write/Glob/Grep.
- System prompt (main.js:20472-20473): 'Read the spec, then use
  curl. Always | jq. For settings PUT, GET-first-then-PUT-whole.'
- The framing 'When users say you, they mean Alma' explicitly
  separates the LLM from the app — the LLM controls the app the
  same way a human would.

WebSocket sync: alma broadcasts state changes from API to all
connected renderer clients. Agent curl → server update → WS push
→ renderer re-renders. Agent doesn't need IPC awar…

subject: PR-CHAT-MARKDOWN-COPY-PENDING-0: gate chat markdown copy feedback
notes: round-4 alma autoCompact full trace (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 22)
body:
Round-2 02 mentioned autoCompact as 'auto-compact in prepareStep'
but never traced. This note covers the full pipeline.

Settings: chat.autoCompact = {enabled, threshold 60-95%,
keepRecentMessages 2-20 USER TURNS, summaryModel? (falls back to
toolModel, then thread's defaultModel)}.

3 trigger sites:
1. Pre-request check (main.js:62150-62330+): runs before each
   user turn; reads last assistant's metadata.usage, compares
   percent to threshold. Proactive — compact before sending.
2. Mid-stream prepareStep (main.js:62997-63095): AI SDK hook
   between tool-calling steps. Catches mid-turn overflow when a
   tool-heavy turn explodes.
3. Manual /api/threads/:id/compact (main.js:57742-57841): user-
   triggered, threshold not checked, validates min 2 messages.

3-tier fallback:
- Tier 1 KE/ZE (main.js:50071-50171): LLM summary via summaryModel
  with structured prompt (Objective/Progress/Key Details/Context/
  Pending markdown skeleton). Wraps in <context_summary>...</...>
  + synthetic assistant ack 'Understood. I have reviewed the
  conversation summary and will continue from here.' Result has
  metadata.isCompactionSummary flag.
- Tier 2 QE (main.js:50172-50210): hard truncation. Drops old
  half entirely, inserts notice '*[Earlier messages (N messages)
  have been removed]*' + same synthetic ack. No LLM call.
- Tier 3 emergency slice in prepareStep (main.js:63081-63093):
  just messages.slice(YE(messages, max(keepRecent, 1))). No
  notice, no ack. We have to make progress.

Critical detail: YE (main.js:50030-50036) counts USER MESSAGES
not raw message count. Walks backwards counting user messages
until hits N, returns split index. So a single user request
spawning 10 tool calls counts as 1 turn — preserves whole turns.

Token budget: jE=32000 is the OUTPUT RESERVE floor. Allowed
input = (contextWindow - min(modelMaxOutput, jE)) * threshold%.
For 128k model at 80%: 76800 not 102400. targetTokenLimit after
compaction is 60% of contextWindow — headroom for next turn.

Anti-loop guard (main.js:62221-62230): if…

notes: round-4 alma memory recall pipeline (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 23)
body:
Round 1 mentioned memory existed; rounds 2-3 never traced it.
This note covers the full Recall + OperateMemory + autoRetrieve
pipeline with the surprise 0.1 similarity threshold default.

Two tools (main.js:17853, 29668):
- Recall: read memories
- OperateMemory: add/update/delete
Both in Yd exact-preserve set (round-3 02) — never compacted.

Defaults (main.js:59679-59705) are all ON and aggressive:
- similarityThreshold: 0.1 (surprise — typical is 0.5-0.7)
- maxRetrievedMemories: 5
- queryRewriting: true (the reason 0.1 works)
- 3 separate model slots: chat / tool / embedding

Pre-turn retrieveMemoriesForContext (main.js:59760-59814):
1. Incognito thread short-circuit — privacy by omission, no
   embedding generation either
2. Settings gate (enabled && autoRetrieve)
3. Embedding provider availability check
4. Optional query rewriting (LLM call)
5. Embed + vector search with limit + threshold + linkedUserIds
6. Format hits for system prompt injection
- onProgress callback emits retrieving/ready stages for UI
- Empty result is silent (no negative injection)

Query rewriter (main.js:59706-59758) is the hidden quality win.
Prompt at main.js:59739 instructs:
- ALWAYS output English regardless of input language
- Map query into 'User's X is Y' subject-verb-object shape that
  memories are stored in
- For broad 'tell me about myself' queries, list multiple
  specific attributes

Examples from prompt:
- '我叫什么' → "User's name is"
- 'What's my job?' → "User works as occupation job"
- '介绍一下我' → "User's name is, User works as, User likes, User lives in"

The fact-shape projector decouples query language from storage
language AND gives embedding distance something specific to
match. That's why 0.1 threshold works without flooding the
agent with noise.

linkedUserIds (main.js:59782) glues bot-platform identity. User
typing in Telegram has different userId than same user in
Discord; people-profile graph (referenced at main.js:62108)
links them. Memory from any linked account counts. Cross-
platform…

subject: PR-ERROR-BOUNDARY-COPY-PENDING-0: gate error report copy feedback
notes: round-4 alma bot integration behavioral contract (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 24)
body:
Rounds 1-3 mentioned Telegram/Discord/Feishu only as bot-source
bypass channels (round-3 04). This note traces the actual
prompt-as-contract + CLI-as-API pattern that turns alma into
a credible group-chat participant.

Strategy: bot integration is NOT a renderer concern. When alma
boots a bot, it ships a ~3.5k-char system prompt (main.js:35107
Telegram, main.js:62108 group prompt) that:
1. Establishes IDENTITY (your name is Alma)
2. Sets FORMAT rules (no Markdown, one sentence per message)
3. Teaches CLI surface (alma group send, alma people append)
4. Encodes BEHAVIOR (proactive help, promise tracking)
5. Encodes PRIVACY firewall

Agent uses the SAME Bash tool it has in desktop mode to invoke
CLI subcommands. No new IPC, no new permission surface.

CLI surface (30+ commands):
- Messaging: alma group send / dm / msg delete / sticker /
  sticker-find / react / send photo|file|audio|video
- Discord bridge: alma discord list / send / dm / send-photo /
  send-file / delete
- Knowledge (4 layers): alma group context / history / search /
  list / rules show|add / pin / leave
- People graph: alma people list / show / set / append
  (profiles at ~/.config/alma/people/<name>.md + avatar.jpg)
- Self-evolution: alma skill search / install,
  alma memory grep

Anti-pattern rules (the prompt's most interesting work):

DUPLICATE MESSAGE PREVENTION: text reply is auto-sent, so if
the agent ALSO uses alma group send to current chat, content
doubles. The detector at main.js:36056-36061 parses the Bash
log for 'alma group send' / 'alma dm' / 'alma msg sticker' and
SUPPRESSES the text reply if matched. Prompt teaches rule;
runtime enforces.

ACTION OVER WORDS + PROMISE TRACKING: 'before each reply,
mentally review: did I promise to do something I haven't done
yet?' Pushes promise-queue bookkeeping into the prompt.

NEVER SAY 'I CAN'T' — SELF-EVOLVE: agent forbidden from saying
'I don't know how'; must run alma skill search / install / use
cycle instead. This operationalizes round-3 01 skills system.

NO MEANINGLESS FILLER: silenc…
