subject: notes: round-4 alma workspaces + project scoping (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 25)
body:
Rounds 1-3 never traced workspaces; round-3 01 saw .alma/skills
as a workspace-scoped path but didn't follow it. This note covers
the table, default resolution cascade, thread binding, and
project-scoped surfaces.

Schema (main.js:257-276): workspaces with isTemporary +
showInList + isWorktree + parentWorkspaceId + worktreeBranch +
autoWorktree + autoWorktreeBaseBranch + prNumber/url/state/base.
Two surprises: worktrees are workspace rows too (with parent
backref), and PR metadata is baked into the workspace row.

Default-workspace resolution (4-layer cascade, replicated at 6
call sites including main.js:19128-19144, 42586, 29033, 43217,
62591, 19138):
1. Per-channel override: settings[platform].channelWorkspaceMap[channelId]
2. User default: settings.general.defaultWorkspaceId
3. Builtin: getOrCreateDefaultWorkspace at main.js:4909-4928
   creates {userData}/workspaces/default
4. Implicit fallthrough if id lookup fails

Threads carry TWO workspace FKs (main.js:286-291):
- workspaceId: session workspace (Bash cwd, Read default, skills)
- artifactWorkspaceId: chat-generated artifacts (lazily created
  as 'Artifacts - thread_title' workspace at main.js:3757-3773)
Both ON DELETE SET NULL — orphan threads survive, tool calls
bail at guard.

Thread reassignment pattern in bot/heartbeat paths (main.js:
34867, 38303, 40676, 42280, 43055): when long-lived thread
recovers, sync it to current default workspace, not the one it
was bound to. Right for bot/cron threads; would be wrong for
code-coupled threads. All call sites are in bot/heartbeat
handlers — verified scoped.

Project-scoped surfaces use workspace.path as prefix:
- <workspace>/.alma/skills (round-3 01 skill loader root #6)
- <workspace>/.alma/agents (cross-ref round-2 07)
- workspacePath passed to Bash session cwd

isInsideSkillDirectory (main.js:18622) treats project-root
.alma/skills equivalently to ~/.config/alma/skills for safety
checks.

ACP/Bash sessions keyed on (workspaceId:threadId) at
main.js:48110-48150. killSessionsForWorkspace iterates and
nukes…

subject: fix: gate first-run quick chat submit
notes: round-4 alma Whisper voice transcription (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 26)
body:
Whisper is voice input for bots (round-4 04) and desktop mic.
Rounds 1-3 never traced it. This note covers the 3-tier dispatch
+ IPC PCM round-trip + language code normalization.

Settings: whisper.{enabled, model: 'base'|'small'|'medium'|
'turbo'|..., language: 'auto'|'en'|'zh'|...}

3-tier dispatch (main.js:36755-36786):
1. Local model (preferred — latency + privacy)
2. OpenAI cloud (fallback when local fails OR not downloaded)
3. null (bot interprets as transcription failed)

Local WhisperService (main.js:34225-34310):
- Wraps @fugood/whisper.node native binding
- Lazy module loading at first use (fast startup)
- ggml model storage at {userData}/whisper_models/ggml-<id>.bin
- isModelDownloaded / getDownloadedModels helpers
- initialize(modelId, language) is single-flight via
  isInitializing flag; old context released before new model
  loads (no RAM leak); useGpu:true with graceful fallback

REST API (main.js:53600-53602):
- GET  /api/whisper/models — list available + downloaded
- POST /api/whisper/models/:modelId/download — fetch a model
operator agent (round-4 01) can curl these too.

IPC PCM decode round-trip (main.js:36830-36874): local Whisper
needs Float32 16kHz PCM but audio arrives encoded (OGG/MP4).
Node lacks Web Audio. Solution: main → renderer message with
encoded buffer + id, renderer decodes via Web Audio API, sends
back PCM Float32. 15s timeout, ID-based matching prevents
crosswire on concurrent decodes, double-resolve guard for
timeout race.

Cloud transcription (main.js:36787-36829):
- FormData POST to api.openai.com/v1/audio/transcriptions
- 'verbose_json' response gets language detection free
- No PCM round-trip — OpenAI accepts encoded directly
- Hardcoded audio/ogg content type (works for Telegram;
  tolerated for Discord MP4)

Language code normalization (main.js:36805-36809): OpenAI
returns mixed ISO codes ('en') and English names ('english'/
'mandarin'). Helper maps to canonical ISO. Critical for the
bot prompt language-match rule (round-4 04 'ALWAYS reply in
same language').…

subject: notes: round-4 alma WebSocket sync architecture (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 27)
body:
Pivoted from NvChad (only settings schema in main.js, impl likely
renderer-side) to WebSocket sync — the architectural through-line
that ties round-4 01/02/03/05 telemetry events together and
backs round-2 10 Chrome Relay transport.

Strategy: single Express server (main.js:65411) piggybacking REST
+ WS on one port. ws via npm 'ws' package (main.js:166). Renderer
discovers port through ~/.config/alma/api-spec.md (round-4 01),
connects to ws://localhost:<port>/ws/<channel>.

9 routed channels at main.js:59107-59320+:
- /ws/threads (interactive: generate_response, stop_generation
  send-side; broadcast: thread CRUD, message_updated, todo,
  context_compaction events)
- /ws/settings (settings_updated broadcast; theme_preview is
  client-to-client echo without DB write — multi-window live
  preview)
- /ws/providers (CRUD events; strips availableModels before
  send to keep payload tight)
- /ws/memory (recall + write events)
- /ws/skills (enable/disable/reorder)
- /ws/mcp-resources (resources_list_changed / resource_updated)
- /ws/debug-sse (diagnostic stream)
- /ws/browser-relay?token=… (Chrome extension transport with
  token auth via Ey.validateToken — round-2 10 cross-ref)
- /ws/terminal/<sessionId> (bidirectional PTY bridge with
  scrollback-on-connect)
- /ws/workspace/<id> (workspace-scoped events with existence
  validation)

Auth model: 127.0.0.1 trust + per-channel exceptions. Only
browser-relay requires token (rotated via REST). Rest is
localhost-trusted same as REST API.

Broadcast helpers (main.js:58990-59014) share template:
- Stamped at broadcast time (clients can ignore stale by
  timestamp)
- Open-state filter, closed sockets cleaned by close handler
- No backpressure handling
- Strip-large-fields pattern: availableModels removed before
  send (huge for OpenAI)

Event vocabulary follows 3 rules: past-tense verbs
(thread_created not create_thread), topic-prefixed clusters,
minimal payloads ({id} for deletes, full object for create/
update — events are notify, not data sync).

theme_preview is …

subject: notes: round-5 opens with ACP bridge full trace (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 28)
body:
Round 4 closed at 7 notes. Round 5 opens with the ACP coder-agent
bridge — round-2 04 and round-3 04 mentioned it as a permission
source but never traced the integration mechanism.

ACP = Agent Client Protocol. JSON-RPC dialect for external coder
agents alma launches as sub-processes. Supported bridges: Claude
Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, Antigravity, generic acp.

Integration via @mcpc-tech/acp-ai-provider (main.js:162) — npm
package wraps spawn + JSON-RPC client + AI SDK provider adapter.
Agent loop sees ACP-backed providers as ordinary LanguageModelV1.

Provider config shape (DB at main.js:462-463):
- acpCommand: executable to spawn
- acpArgs: CLI args
- acpMcpServerIds: forward Maka MCP servers to ACP runtime
- acpAuthMethodId: bridge-side login method id
- acpModelMapping: Claude 3-tier (opus/sonnet/haiku) + subagent
  slot

Session lifecycle: keyed on (providerId, threadId). Sessions hold
provider instance + availableCommands + handlerSetUp flag +
config.requestThreadId.

setupSessionUpdateHandler (main.js:19475-19704) is the centerpiece
— intercept-and-forward subscription proxy:
1. Wrap underlying client's setSessionUpdateHandler
2. On every ACP notification, run alma's interceptor FIRST:
   - available_commands_update → cache + notify alma listeners
   - tool_call_update → synthesize toolExecutionResultCallback
     for alma's UI
3. THEN forward to whatever AI SDK adapter's downstream handler
4. Anti-double-wrap guard via session.handlerSetUp flag
5. 'Controller is already closed' error silent suppression for
   shutdown races (matches err.message AND err.data.details for
   SDK wrappers that nest)

tool_call_update bridging:
- toolName = title || toolCallId fallback
- output = rawOutput ?? content (different bridges, different
  fields)
- status === 'failed' → isError true normalization
- Other statuses pass through

Permission delegation (main.js:19554-19704) is the most
interesting part. ACP-side agent calls setPermissionRequestHandler
when it needs approval. Alma delegates BACK to its own lh(…

notes: round-5 Sprint Harness Planner→Generator→Evaluator (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 29)
body:
Round-2 07 mentioned harness mode in Task tool description but
never traced. This note covers the full pipeline triggered by
handoff.harness.enabled: true.

User-facing: invisible. The Task tool description (main.js:22269)
explicitly says NEVER mention 'harness', 'sprints', 'contracts',
'evaluator' to user — internal implementation details.

4 DB tables (main.js:336-410):
- agent_missions: harnessMode='sprint-harness', currentSprintId,
  currentPhase, maxIterations (default 5), specArtifactPath
- agent_runs: harnessRole ('planner'|'generator'|'evaluator'),
  sprintId
- mission_sprints: sprintNumber, title, description, agentId,
  status
- sprint_contracts: version, criteria JSON, negotiationLog,
  status — split from sprints so contracts can evolve
- sprint_evaluations: attemptNumber, generatorRunId,
  evaluatorRunId, grades JSON, overallPassed, feedbackSummary

Filesystem artifacts at {missionDir}/:
- spec.md (planner's human-readable markdown)
- sprints.json (machine-readable sprint plan with criteria)
- sprints/sprint-N/generator-output.md (per attempt)
- sprints/sprint-N/evaluation-M.json (per attempt)
- sprints/sprint-N/evaluation.json (canonical latest)

Phase 1 Planner (main.js:22002-22066): subagentType 'Plan',
agentProfileId 'planner', harnessRole 'planner'. Prompt
(main.js:22012) breaks user brief into 3-10 sprints with 3-7
criteria each. Each criterion has {id, description, threshold
0-100, weight}. Must use Write tool to emit sprints.json — if
missing, mission fails hard with explicit error.

Phase 2 Generator (main.js:21745-21767): subagentType 'coder',
agentProfileId from sprint.agentId or 'developer', harnessRole
'generator'. Per sprint, up to maxIterationsPerSprint attempts
(default 5). Output to generator-output.md per attempt. Retry
feeds previous evaluator feedback back into next attempt's
prompt — round-trip the failed criteria so generator knows what
to fix.

Phase 3 Evaluator (main.js:21769-21900): subagentType
'general-purpose', agentProfileId 'evaluator', harnessRole
'evaluator'. D…

subject: fix: gate sidebar session row actions
notes: round-5 Hummingbird screen-context capture (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 30)
body:
Round-4 07 saw hummingbirdContext on /ws/threads generate_response
payload as a mystery field. This note traces the full Quick Chat
screen-context capture pipeline.

Strategy: Hummingbird is 'what's on screen right now' snapshot for
Quick Chat. User invokes Quick Chat global hotkey → alma captures
three things:
1. Frontmost app (name, bundle id, window title, URL)
2. User's text selection (highlighted text = deictic target)
3. AX tree of focused window (raw visible content)

Wrapped in LittleBird-style XML tags, injected into agent prompt
with explicit guidance.

Renderer can pre-capture and ship in WS message; if absent, main
process captures fresh (main.js:60790-60878):

3-step pipeline with 300ms total timeout:
1. $T(timeoutMs: min(200, remaining)) → frontmost app via NSWorkspace
2. xT(pid, {timeoutMs: remaining, maxChars: 5000, bundleId, url}) →
   AX tree traversal
3. Classify source: ax|browser|partial|empty

Key details:
- Hard 300ms total — Quick Chat must FEEL instant
- 5k char cap on AX content
- remaining() recomputes budget after each step
- permissionDenied propagates when macOS Accessibility not granted

Prompt guidance (main.js:62807) teaches 6 things:
1. 'LittleBird-style' tag dialect named so model doesn't confuse
   with similar tags
2. Deictic priority: text-selection > front-app window+url >
   context dump > nothing
3. 'Read but do not echo' — prevents leaking context to user
4. 'Quote short spans when confirming' — bridge to user
5. Refer by description ('your Chrome window'), not tag name
6. Staleness rule — snapshot invalidates after tool calls

Most subtle/important: rule 6. Without it model cites stale
content after Browser.click() / Bash modifications.

4-way source classification:
- ax: AX traversal worked, content present → full <context>
- browser: URL is best context → emphasize url attr
- partial: AX returned but empty → frontApp only
- empty: couldn't read anything → just app name

Lets model right-size confidence rather than binary yes/no.

LittleBird refere…

subject: notes: round-5 alma safeStorage encryption truth (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 31)
body:
Round-4 01 noted the api-spec's 'Provider API keys are stored
encrypted' claim. This note traces what's actually encrypted vs
plaintext and surfaces the gap with the marketing.

Primitive: Electron safeStorage (main.js:37). Platform backends:
- macOS: Keychain
- Windows: DPAPI
- Linux: libsecret (gnome-keyring / kwallet5)

3 API calls used: encryptString / decryptString /
isEncryptionAvailable.

4 call sites with same template (save: check available → encrypt
else plain; load: same symmetric check):
- main.js:15554-15596 Copilot account tokens
- main.js:15580-15596 token save/load
- main.js:16229-16261 Claude subscription tokens
- main.js:24727-24739 MCP OAuth (base64-wrapped for DB storage)

3 cross-cutting observations:
1. isEncryptionAvailable() checked at BOTH save AND load — must
   match. Risk: data migration across systems with different
   encryption capability silently corrupts.
2. Base64 wrapper pattern (pf/mf at main.js:24730-24738)
   invariant-preserves: same output shape whether encryption
   available or not. Used when blob must live in TEXT
   contexts (DB column, env var, JSON artifact).
3. Plaintext fallback is SILENT — no log, no warning, no file
   marker distinguishing encrypted vs plain on disk. Reasonable
   UX tradeoff (Linux without libsecret still works) but no
   audit trail.

CRITICAL finding: provider.apiKey column (main.js:451) is stored
PLAINTEXT in SQLite, NOT encrypted via safeStorage. This
contradicts the api-spec note 'Provider API keys are stored
encrypted.' The encrypted-via-safeStorage paths are for OAuth
tokens (Copilot, Claude subscription, MCP OAuth) — NOT for the
provider api_key column.

Verified via all call sites that read t.apiKey directly
(main.js:1188 / 12560 / 14573 / 16426 / 16430 / 16551 / 16814 /
16839) — none decrypt first. Storage model is linear:
SQLite plaintext → t.apiKey → Bearer header.

Provider broadcast helper (main.js:59010-59014) only strips
availableModels — NOT apiKey. So the api-spec's 'do not expose'
claim has no scrub layer I can…

subject: notes: round-5 alma provider abstraction taxonomy (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 34)
body:
Round-4 01 documented 15 provider types from api-spec. Round-5 01
covered ACP. This note covers the remaining 14 non-ACP types
through the unified hd() factory.

Factory: hd(provider, modelId) at main.js:16423-16599. Single
async function returns AI SDK LanguageModelV1 regardless of
provider type. Cross-cuts EVERY agent loop in alma.

Universal pattern: switch on provider.type → call relevant AI SDK
factory (createOpenAI / createAnthropic / createGoogleGenerativeAI
/ createOpenAICompatible / etc.) → pass apiKey + baseURL + fetch:
dd → invoke with modelId.

CRITICAL: universal fetch hook dd = Tr(er) at main.js:16421.
Every provider's outgoing HTTPS call goes through dd. Means:
- Proxy settings apply to ALL providers uniformly
- Retry settings apply uniformly
- Sentry HTTP instrumentation (round-5 06) wraps everything
Without it: alma would thread proxy config through every
provider's SDK individually.

5-pattern taxonomy:

Pattern 1 OpenAI direct (5 providers): openai/anthropic/google/
openrouter/deepseek. Each has dedicated @ai-sdk/<vendor>
package. baseURL || undefined fallthrough to SDK default.

Pattern 2 OpenAI-compatible (5 providers): moonshot/
zai-coding-plan/ollama/volcengine/custom. createOpenAICompatible
with different baseURLs. ollama gets apiKey || 'ollama' sentinel.
custom adds customHeaders JSON + useMaxCompletionTokens fetch
wrapper that renames max_tokens to max_completion_tokens.

Pattern 3 Anthropic-compatible (2 providers): kimi-coding-plan
adds anthropic-beta header for interleaved thinking + fine-
grained tool streaming. claude-subscription is the most
elaborate — empty apiKey + custom fetch that calls
getAccessToken() + buildCloakedRequest (round-2 09) + Bearer
auth.

Pattern 4 unique implementations:
- azure (main.js:16452-16469): extracts resource name by
  stripping protocol + .openai.azure.com from baseURL.
  isResponseAPI flag selects provider.responses(modelId) vs
  provider.chat(modelId). apiVersion default '2024-08-01-
  preview'.
- aihubmix (main.js:16445-16451): wraps fetch…

subject: notes: round-6 opens with title generation pipeline (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 35)
body:
Rounds 1-5 closed at 37 source-grounded notes. Round 6 opens
with focused notes on smaller subsystems that thread through
already-covered areas.

generateThreadTitle (main.js:59815-59916) fires async after
first agent turn on a new thread — title appears in sidebar
while user can already start the next turn.

Context window: first 4 messages × 500 chars (main.js:59855-
59861). ~600 tokens total — cheap. Per-message 500-char cap
prevents tool result stack traces from swamping the prompt.

Tool model resolution (main.js:59820-59838):
- Pl() resolves effective tool model (NEVER user's chat model
  — cheap-and-fast slot)
- isAutoDetected flag logged for debug ('wrong model picked'
  is common complaint)
- Silent bail if no model — user sees default 'New Chat' not
  toast spam

Prompt (main.js:59870-59871) — 5 teaching moves:
1. Length range '3-8 words' (concrete > 'concise')
2. 'Not generic' counter-conditions against 'Question about X'
3. Language matching with examples (same as round-4 04 bot
   prompt; IMPORTANT prefix makes non-negotiable)
4. 'Do NOT use quotes' (model wraps in quotes despite request
   — belt-and-braces with line 59883 strip)
5. 'Do NOT include any explanation' (forbids preamble fluff)

Validation (main.js:59883-59903):
- Strip wrapping quotes
- Length > 0 AND <= 100 chars (rejection silent)
- 100-char cap = ~25 words, far more than 3-8 prompted;
  tolerates verbosity but prevents runaway corrupting DB

3-broadcast UX sequence over /ws/threads:
- title_generating before LLM call (shimmer in sidebar)
- title_generated on success (replace with new title)
- title_generating cleared on failure or rejection (silent
  revert to 'New Chat')

Failure handling (main.js:59905-59914): try/catch wraps all
errors with duration log, broadcasts cleared spinner, returns
null. Thread keeps default title; user can manually rename.

Lightweight perf telemetry via [TitleGen] namespaced console
logs with elapsed seconds at each checkpoint. With Sentry only
10% sampled (round-5 06), these logs are local …

subject: fix: guard clipboard feedback after unmount
notes: round-6 alma skill extraction meta-loop (WAWQAQ 10min loop round 36)
body:
Round-3 01 mentioned the skill extraction meta-loop but only
excerpted the prompt. This note traces analyzeAndExtractSkill
end-to-end.

Trigger: async after each agent turn. Two gates BOTH must pass.

Gate 1 — cumulative tool-call floor (main.js:59939-59947):
Conversation needs ≥5 cumulative tool-invocation parts before
extraction is considered. Configurable via
skillExtraction.minToolCalls.

Gate 2 — since-last-analysis delta (main.js:59948-59957):
If we already analyzed at tool-call count N, won't re-analyze
until ≥N more tool calls happen. Watermark stored on message
metadata (skillExtractionAnalyzed + skillExtractionToolCallCount)
— durable across app restarts.

Settings (main.js:59917-59930): both enabled (default true) and
minToolCalls (default 5). Privacy escape: incognito threads
skip — same isIncognito short-circuit pattern as memory recall
(round-4 03).

Context shape (main.js:59976-60029) is ASYMMETRIC:
- Earlier messages (all but last 8): 300 chars/msg max, plain
  'User: …' / 'Assistant: …' / '[Tool: name]' format
- Recent (last 8): 3000 chars/msg max, bold-prefixed
  '**User:**' / '**Assistant:**' / '**Tool:**' format
- Total cap: 18000 chars with hard truncate

The asymmetry biases the analyzer toward 'this is the pattern'
state — skill workflows usually crystallize at the END.
Bold-prefix on recent section weights model attention there.

Same toolModel cascade as title gen (round-6 01) — Pl() → wd()
→ fd(). Third pipeline using cheap-and-fast tool model
(autoCompact summary + memory query rewriting + title gen +
this). Architectural rule: auxiliary AI pipelines NEVER use
user's chat model.

Existing-skills injection (main.js:60055-60064): analyzer sees
all current skills as 'name: description' lines in system
prompt. Output JSON includes existingSkillToUpdate field so
analyzer can recommend MERGING vs creating duplicates.

JSON salvage pattern (main.js:60067-60083): regex-extract first
{...} block. Tolerates model wrapping in code fences / preamble
despite prompt rules.

Wo…

notes: round-6 alma capability discovery 4-tier cascade (WAWQAQ 10min loop catch-up)
body:
Round-5 07 showed providers unified behind one factory. This note
covers HOW alma knows which models support vision / functionCalling
/ imageOutput / reasoning / contextWindow — 4-tier cascade.

Why it matters: capability resolution runs HOT on every
send-response flow. Each capability gates behavior:
- vision → user can attach images
- imageOutput → route to artifact pane
- functionCalling → tools available in turn
- streaming → progressive vs all-at-once render
- contextWindow → autoCompact threshold math (round-4 02)

Default Oc = {streaming: true} (main.js:13355) — safe assumption
when nothing known.

Tier 1 — Regex override patterns (main.js:13359-13396, the Mc
array): 5 hardcoded special cases mostly image-generation models
(nano-banana, gemini-image, grok-image, gpt-image, model-router).
All set functionCalling:false to prevent tool-call errors.
Image-output models typically streaming:false too — single image
not token stream.

Tier 2 — In-memory cache Sc with dual key (main.js:13289):
- bare modelId ('gpt-4o')
- provider-qualified 'providerId:modelId' ('openai:gpt-4o')
Both keys insert SAME capabilities. Disambiguates cross-provider
name collisions.
10-minute TTL (6e5 ms). Single-flight via Ac promise — refresh
in-progress is awaited, not duplicated. Stale-while-revalidate
when stale data available.

Tier 3 — DB cache (main.js:13313-13338): warmup loads
getAllModelCapabilitiesCache rows. fetchedAt set to OLDEST
entry's timestamp — conservative (if anything is stale, refresh
everything).

Tier 4 — models.dev/api.json (main.js:13270): community-curated
public capabilities catalog. Free, no auth. Alma fetches every
10 min when cache stale. Response transformed by _c() normalizer
into alma's shape, persisted to DB.

Resolution function Dc (main.js:13402-13438):
1. Empty input → Oc default
2. Tier 1 regex pattern match → return with Pc overrides
3. Cache lookup → return with Pc overrides
4. Longest-substring fallback: if 'gpt-4o-2024-08-06' unknown
   but 'gpt-4o' is, use gpt-…

subject: feat(browser): embedded browser automation with a visible-conversation lease
body:
Port PawWork's opencli CDP + numbered-ref observe→act approach into the
desktop agent. The agent drives a real, per-conversation Chromium view and
the page renders live in a right-side panel.

- Sealed CDP bridge (cdp-bridge.ts): wraps webContents.debugger behind a
  loopback WebSocket whose secret stays in main-process memory and never
  crosses IPC; opencli's stealth script auto-registers on connect.
- Six generic observe→act tools (navigate / snapshot / click / type / wait /
  extract): numbered [ref] snapshot to observe, act by ref. Takeover-reload is
  deferred to the first mutate so observing never disturbs a page the user has
  open; browser_extract treats an invalid selector as "no match".
- Per-conversation WebContentsView (controller + view-manager), the renderer
  panel that mirrors its on-screen strip each frame, and the main/preload IPC
  wiring. Views are ephemeral across restart; a shared persistent partition
  keeps logins, with a once-per-partition security backstop.
- Visible-conversation lease: the agent touches the browser only for the
  conversation on screen. EVERY action is rejected when its conversation is
  backgrounded; a mutate also needs a real on-screen viewport (native CDP
  clicks hit-test a composited frame a hidden view lacks). The lease is
  continuous and revocable — an action still running when the user switches
  away is severed — and background throttling tracks shown-ness.

Includes the main-process unit tests (cdp-bridge, session, logic, tools,
view-manager, automation-host) driven through fakes, no live CDP endpoint.

subject: feat(permission): dedicated browser permission category + honest turn-wide prompt
body:
Browser effects are irreversible, so they get their own permission category
instead of riding the read/exec defaults:

- `browser` is prompt-on-effect: blocked in explore, prompts in ask AND
  execute (never auto-allowed like read tools).
- It carries its own prompt reason (the dialog names the logged-in session it
  drives, not a generic "custom" request) and ONE turn-wide permission scope,
  so "allow for this turn" carries the whole observe→act loop instead of
  re-prompting on every ref.
- The prompt note (shown only when "remember for this turn" is checked, since
  the grant only persists then) is honest that one allow covers the turn's
  reads, navigation, clicks, and typing — the live visible view is the
  act-phase safety net, so there is no second prompt.

Also keeps the chat-header badges from collapsing into vertical text when the
browser panel narrows the chat column.

subject: test(browser): real Electron observe→act + visible-lease smoke; pin opencli
body:
- browser-observe-act-smoke.mjs: real Electron E2E (sealed ws bridge ↔
  webContents.debugger ↔ opencli ↔ live DOM): goto → numbered snapshot →
  fill-by-ref → click-by-ref → DOM-effect verify → markdown extract; the
  partition backstop installing once across views; and the visible lease end
  to end through the real host/manager/BrowserSession — background read /
  navigate / mutate rejected with no view or connection, a mutate waiting out
  a modal-close viewport restore, an in-flight read revoked against the live
  bridge on switch-away, and background throttling restored on hide.
- Pin @jackwener/opencli to exact 1.8.4 (+ lockfile) so the opencli-contract
  test's "pinned release" assertion can't drift on a future 1.8.x.

subject: feat(lab): @maka/lab walking skeleton — headless Config × Task → score (#31)
body:
First end-to-end slice of the agent experiment lab: run one Config
against one Task in an isolated sandbox, capture the trajectory, score
it, and return a ResultRecord. De-risks the integration (headless
SessionManager run + sandbox + evaluator composing into one real run);
matrix/compare/CLI and real-model backends are later additions.

- contracts.ts: Task (instruction + fixture workspace + verification
  command), Config (backend/connection/model), ResultRecord. Minimal;
  systemPrompt/Execution/SWE-bench-pack ingestion deferred.
- sandbox.ts: prepareWorkspace copies the fixture to a throwaway dir so
  the agent never mutates the source (isolation, not asking).
- evaluator.ts: runVerification runs the Task's test command in the
  throwaway workspace after the agent finishes (config can't grade
  itself), with output cap + timeout kill.
- runner.ts: runExperiment wires a pure-Node SessionManager (injected
  registerBackends keeps model/credential wiring out of the lab core),
  drives one turn, captures the InvocationResult trajectory via
  runtimeInvocationObserver, scores it, returns a ResultRecord.
- tests: FakeBackend e2e (pass + fail fixtures, trajectory persisted as
  runtime-events.jsonl), sandbox isolation, evaluator pass/fail/timeout.

Independent of the credential migration (#32/#33): the skeleton runs on
FakeBackend and needs no credentials.

subject: feat(lab): matrix + comparison + CLI + real ai-sdk backend (#31)
body:
Completes the MVP loop on top of the walking skeleton: run a grid of
Configs × Tasks, persist canonical results, and compare.

- matrix.ts: runMatrix runs the full cross product (sequential; a thrown
  run becomes a failed cell instead of aborting the grid) with a
  per-cell onResult callback.
- results.ts: ResultRecord JSONL is canonical truth; toComparisonTable
  derives a git-diffable markdown grid (tasks × configs, ✅/❌/⚠️,
  pass-rate footer). ResultRecord gains an optional `error`.
- backends.ts: the two concrete backend wirings, kept out of the engine.
  registerFakeBackend (deterministic). registerAiSdkBackend resolves a
  Config's slug against spec connections, reads the API key from a named
  env var (no secrets at rest), and wires a minimal AiSdkBackend (model +
  builtin tools + execute-mode permission). Telemetry/artifact/synthesis
  hooks omitted — a benchmark scores via the verification command.
- runner.ts: the drain loop now auto-approves permission requests — a
  headless benchmark has no human to confirm; throwaway-workspace
  isolation is the safety net.
- cli.ts: `maka-lab run <spec.json> [--out <dir>]` and
  `maka-lab compare <results.jsonl>`; task fixtures resolve relative to
  the spec. Exposed as the `maka-lab` bin.
- tests (15 total): CLI end-to-end smoke (spawns the built bin on a fake
  spec → results.jsonl + comparison.md → compare), matrix cross-product
  + failed-cell, JSONL round-trip, table rendering.

The real ai-sdk backend is typecheck-verified but not unit-tested (a live
model call is non-deterministic and costs money); it needs a live smoke
test with a real API key. Everything else is deterministic and green.

subject: docs(lab): README + runnable fake-backend demo spec (#31)
body:
README documents the Config × Task model, the CLI (run/compare), the
spec shape (incl. a real ai-sdk connection with apiKeyEnv), the two
backends, and what's deliberately out of MVP scope. examples/demo.spec.json
+ examples/demo/marker.txt run green on the fake backend with no API key:

  maka-lab run examples/demo.spec.json --out /tmp/maka-lab-demo

subject: test(lab): real ai-sdk coding-task example (validated live, #31)
body:
examples/fix-add — a buggy add() with a failing node:test. A real model
run must read the files, fix src.mjs, and turn `node --test` green.

Live smoke confirmed end-to-end on DeepSeek (deepseek-chat): completed,
passed, exit 0, 124-event trajectory using Edit/Write/Bash; the source
fixture stayed buggy (the agent only touched the throwaway copy), proving
sandbox isolation + headless permission auto-approve work against a real
backend. README points at it as the canonical real-run example.

subject: Merge pull request #34 from likun666661/deepseek-tool-source-economy-v1
body: Add Tool Source Economy v1

subject: fix(lab): address Codex review — permission policy, sandbox, evaluator, table (#36)
body:
- runner: stop blanket-approving permission prompts. Allow ordinary tool
  use, DENY dangerous categories (fs_destructive / git_destructive /
  privileged / browser) by default — the workspace is a copy, not a jail,
  so a tool can still escape via absolute paths/network. Opt in with
  allowDangerousTools (real sandbox only). Corrects the misleading
  "workspace is the safety net" comment. Also: a run that didn't complete
  can no longer read as passed.
- evaluator: spawn detached + SIGKILL the process group on timeout, so
  backgrounded grandchildren die too (a plain child.kill leaked them).
- sandbox: reject fixture symlinks (fs.cp preserved them verbatim → escape
  to source/host) and clean up the temp dir if the copy fails (it was
  leaked before the runner's finally registered).
- results: cellKey was separated by a NUL byte, making results.ts a binary
  file to git — switch to a JSON key. Render a failed run as ⚠️ (distinct
  from ❌ verification-failed) and exclude it from the pass count. Escape
  `|`/newline in ids so they can't break the table.
- cli: reject unknown flags and require a value after --out.
- README: honest permission/safety wording.

+5 tests (symlink reject, failed-run render, id escaping, unknown flag,
missing flag value); 20 total green. Cross-process credential lock and a
real container sandbox remain deliberately out of scope.

subject: Merge pull request #35 from linc77/codex/fix-runtime-ledger-refresh-race
body: [codex] fix runtime ledger refresh race

subject: feat(runtime): deferred tool loading core — exposure gating, load_tool, same-turn prepareStep
body:
Layer 1 mechanism (issue #15). Heavy-schema tools (Rive/Office/browser) can be withheld
from the per-turn prompt and loaded on demand, keeping the cached provider prefix lean.

- MakaTool.exposure 'direct'|'deferred'; canonicalizeToolSet trims deferred-and-unloaded
  tools from activeTools while keeping providerTools full for dispatch.
- load_tool: always-on namespace catalog (cards = name + one-line summary), thin
  { loaded:[...] } result (no schema), provider-neutral lookup (not search).
- deferred-activation + ModelAdapter prepareStep forward: per-step active set derived from
  prior steps' load_tool calls, so a tool loaded at step N is advertised at step N+1.

Proven: MockLanguageModelV3 wire-trim (a deferred schema never crosses the wire) and an
end-to-end same-turn load->use test (Codex Δ1). 20 new tests; runtime suite 499/500
(the 1 failure is a pre-existing env-dependent network-proxy test).

Pending: live AiSdkBackend wiring (execute-guard, active-subset diagnostics, durable
cross-turn seed) and main.ts registration + token measurement.

subject: feat(runtime): diagnostics measure the provider-visible (active) tool subset
body:
Codex Δ3: toolSchemaHash and toolSchemaCharsForDiagnostics now hash/measure
only the active (provider-visible) subset, not the full providerTools registry.
Without this, an inactive deferred tool's schema would over-count tokens in #19
and a change to an unadvertised schema would false-fire tool_schema_changed.
A genuine load still moves the hash and reports tool_schema_changed.

3 new tests in request-shape.test.ts (7/7 green).

subject: feat(runtime): wire deferred tool loading into AiSdkBackend (guard + durable seed)
body:
Slices 5/7/8 (runtime side):
- S5 execute-boundary guard: ToolRuntime gains a per-step active snapshot
  (setStepActivation) and rejects a deferred tool absent from it — before
  permission eval and the real impl. Uses the step-start snapshot, not a
  cumulative set, so same-step parallel load_tool(x)+x rejects x (Codex Δ5).
  Closes the AI SDK activeTools leak (vercel/ai#8653). Recoverable message.
- S7 durable cross-turn seed: seedNamespacesFromRuntimeEvents reconstructs the
  loaded-set from committed load_tool function_call events in the ledger, so a
  tool loaded earlier this session is re-advertised at turn start. Survives
  compaction/recovery; aborted loads never reach committed history (Codex Δ4).
- S8 backend wiring: AiSdkBackendInput.deferredCatalog drives a per-turn
  prepareStep (seed ∪ this-turn loads), feeds the guard snapshot via
  onActiveSnapshot, and seeds step-0 activeTools + diagnostics consistently.
  Inert when no catalog is configured (legacy behavior unchanged).

Tests: 4 guard (ToolRuntime), 3 durable-seed, 3 live-backend integration
(hide-unloaded, cross-turn re-advertise, same-step parallel reject). Suite
512/513 (the 1 fail is the pre-existing network-proxy env test).

subject: feat(desktop): register deferred tool loading — defer Rive/Office/browser via load_tool
body:
Tags the heavy-schema families (RiveWorkflow, OfficeDocument×2, browser×6) as
exposure:'deferred', builds the 3-namespace catalog (rive/office/browser) from
the real tool names, registers buildLoadTool(catalog), and threads
deferredCatalog into AiSdkBackend so their schemas stay off the per-turn wire
until the model loads them on demand.

Kill-switch: set MAKA_DISABLE_DEFERRED_TOOLS to any value to advertise every
tool every turn (no exposure tag, no load_tool, inert guard).

Authoritative measurement (core+explore+heavy): ~2,143 tokens saved per idle
turn (12,358→3,788 schema chars, −69.3%); load_tool catalog costs 762 chars.

Desktop main suite 1469/1469.

subject: fix(runtime): repair deferred tool calls against the current-step active snapshot
body:
The repair callback passed first-step `activeTools` (captured before any
mid-turn load) as the set to match a case-drifted tool name against. Once a
deferred namespace is loaded, `prepareStep` expands the provider-visible tools
for later steps, but the repair list did not follow — so a provider that emitted
a mis-cased loaded deferred name (e.g. `BROWSER_CLICK` after loading `browser`)
no longer matched the canonical tool and was wrongly routed to `invalid`, even
though that tool was advertised in that step.

Follow the current step's active snapshot for the repair set (falling back to
the static active set when no deferred catalog is configured). Adds a live
AiSdkBackend regression: load browser at step 0, emit `BROWSER_CLICK` at step 1,
assert it repairs to canonical `browser_click` and runs instead of routing to
`invalid`.

Addresses the Codex review [P2] on PR #30.

subject: fix(runtime): honest same-turn load diagnostics + self-protecting no-catalog path
body:
Addresses two findings from a GPT-Pro review of PR #30.

P2 — same-turn deferred loads were invisible to cost/cache telemetry. send()
computed toolSchemaChars/hash and the request-shape diagnostic once at stream
start from the step-0 active set, but prepareStep expands the provider tool set
on later steps when a family is loaded this turn. So the first turn that called
load_tool under-reported the heavy schema it actually sent on step N+1, and the
cache reset surfaced a turn late. Extract a computeTurnDiagnostics(active)
closure (one source of truth), report the step-0 view in the stream-start trace
(literally what the first request carries), then refine the durable cost record
+ prefix baseline against the final active set once the stream is consumed —
a no-op on the common no-load path (the ratchet only grows the set, so an
unchanged length means nothing loaded). Next turn now classifies as stable
instead of tool_schema_changed.

P3 — the no-catalog path was not self-protecting. canonicalizeToolSet() hides
any exposure:'deferred' tool when called without a loaded-names set, but
AiSdkBackendInput documents an absent deferredCatalog as "every tool advertised".
A deferred tag with no catalog would strand the tool off the wire with no
load_tool to recover it. Make the contract self-enforcing: with no catalog,
seed all deferred tool names as loaded (advertise everything). Document the
loadedDeferredNames contract on canonicalizeToolSet so direct callers aren't
bitten.

Tests (deferred-tools-backend.test.ts): a same-turn load_tool(browser) records
tool-schema cost for the expanded set (Read+load_tool+browser_click), not the
lean step-0 set; a deferred-tagged tool with no catalog is still advertised at
step 0.

subject: feat(ui): friendly localized presentation for load_tool
body:
The always-on deferred-loading catalog tool surfaced as its raw name
`load_tool` with a raw `{ loaded: [...] }` JSON result card. Give it a
friendly, locale-aware presentation that follows the existing detectUiLocale
zh/en preference:

- display name: 加载工具组 / "Load tools"
- result card: 已加载 <ns> 工具组 / 新增 N 个可用工具：… / 下一步即可调用
  ("Loaded <ns> tool group" / "N tools now available: …" / "Ready to use on
  the next step"), falling back to the generic JSON preview on an unexpected
  shape (e.g. a load failure, which is a text/error result).

Pure renderer change — no backend/runtime types touched. The card reads the
namespace from the call args and the tool list from the result. Copy logic is
extracted as pure functions (describeLoadToolResult / loadToolDisplayName) and
unit-tested in the desktop suite.

subject: fix(runtime): keep high-water "after" hash consistent across a same-turn load
body:
The rebase grafted main's context-budget high-water block onto step-0
diagnostics: it set highWaterRequestShapeHashAfter from the step-0 request shape
before streaming. But a same-turn deferred load later refines the request shape /
prompt segments to the final active set without touching that nested high-water
hash — leaving "after" describing step-0 while the recorded requestShapeHash
describes the final set. Internally inconsistent diagnostic metadata.

Centralize publishing: a single publishTurnDiagnostics() updates the cost record,
the prefix baseline, AND the high-water "after" hash together, so they can never
diverge. The "before" hash is the pre-turn baseline, set once at step 0; the
post-stream refinement re-publishes the final snapshot.

Test: wrap buildPriorMessages to inject a high-water marker (real high-water
reasons need the synthesis-cache subsystem's valid blocks + matching history,
which would brittly couple the test to it) + a same-turn load_tool, then assert
highWaterRequestShapeHashAfter equals the final recorded requestShapeHash.

Addresses the GPT-Pro Web review [P3].

subject: fix(renderer): queue parallel permission requests so none get stranded
body:
A model firing several tool calls in one step (e.g. browser_snapshot +
browser_extract in parallel) produces multiple permission_request events for one
session. The renderer kept a single pending request per session, so the later
request overwrote the earlier one — the overwritten request could never be
answered, its tool stayed parked forever while run status flipped back to
"running", and the turn hung until the user force-stopped it. Confirmed in two
ON-session ledgers: snapshot's request f2d2b2b7 never got a decision, the user
hit stop, snapshot failed with errorClass "Permission".

Replace the single slot with a per-session FIFO queue: parallel requests all
survive, the user clears them one at a time (queue head = active overlay),
nothing is stranded. Pure helpers (enqueue dedups by requestId, dequeue promotes
the next, clear drops all, activePermissionFor reads the head) live in @maka/ui,
unit-tested including the exact stranded-snapshot scenario.

Surfaced while testing deferred tool loading (PR #30): deferred is only the
trigger — loading a whole group makes the model batch-call it in parallel, which
exposes this pre-existing single-slot bug. Deferred activation itself is correct.

- packages/ui/src/permission-queue.ts: FIFO helpers + barrel export
- apps/desktop/src/renderer/main.tsx: permissionBySession -> queue
- tests: permission-queue.test.ts (6); update complete-case source contract to
  assert clearPermissions(...)

subject: fix: address three Codex P2 review findings on PR #30
body:
A Codex review of the PR surfaced three advisory (no P1) issues on top of the
parallel-permission-queue fix. Fixed together rather than carried as debt.

1. permission-engine: allow + rememberForTurn now absorbs other already-parked
   requests sharing the same scope. browser_* shares one turn-scope, so when a
   parallel batch (snapshot + extract + ...) all park and the user answers one
   with "remember for this turn", the rest must not each re-prompt.
   recordResponse resolves the same-scope parked promises; each tool's own
   coroutine emits its own permission_decision_ack, so the UI queue drains
   without a second click.

2. renderer: drain a stale permission-queue entry on tool_result. A permission
   that ends without a user decision (runtime timeout / expiry) emits a
   tool_result, not a permission_decision_ack -- so the FIFO queue could surface
   an already-expired request as an un-answerable overlay. The tool_result
   handler now dequeues by toolUseId (new dequeuePermissionByToolUseId helper);
   no-op on the normal allow/deny path where the ack already dequeued.

3. ai-sdk-backend: telemetry toolCount now counts the active (provider-visible)
   tool subset instead of the full registry, matching toolSchemaChars. Under
   deferred loading the cost/diagnostic record now reflects the on-demand drop.

Tests: permission-engine (absorb same-scope; don't absorb without remember or
across scopes), permission-queue (dequeueByToolUseId drains + no-ops). Full
typecheck clean; runtime affected suites 72/72; desktop permission + contract
tests green.

subject: feat(lab): clean-room grading + honest no-isolation docs (review #36 P1)
body:
The throwaway workspace stops a run from mutating the source fixture, but
it is NOT a security sandbox and a config could rewrite its own test to
pass. This addresses the two open review P1s without ripping out the real
backend (kept usable for your-own-models-on-trusted-tasks):

- Clean-room grading (verification integrity): Task.verification.protectedPaths
  lists the grading assets; the runner restores them from the pristine
  fixture AFTER the agent finishes and BEFORE the verification command runs,
  so a model that rewrote its own test has that edit reverted. Each path is
  removed first (drops an agent-planted symlink) and rejected if it escapes
  the workspace. examples/fix-add now protects test.mjs.
- Honest docs (host isolation): the README states plainly that a real run
  executes tool calls on your machine with your privileges — same exposure
  as running Maka directly — so run only models/tasks you trust. Per-run
  container isolation (mount workspace only, env allowlist, network policy)
  is the named next hardening; allowDangerousTools is for inside it.

Tests (+4, 24 total green): restoreProtectedPaths unit (reverts protected,
keeps the rest, rejects escapes) + a malicious TamperBackend integration
proving protectedPaths reverts the cheat (passed=false) while the unguarded
task lets the cheat win (passed=true) — the guard is load-bearing. The
integration grades via `node check.mjs` (exit-code), not `node --test`, so
the verification child doesn't collide with the lab's own test runner.

subject: test(runtime): align economy toolCount assertions to active-subset semantics
body:
PR #30's P2 #3 redefines promptSegment.toolCount as the model-visible
(active) tool count, not the full providerTools registry (which includes
the invalid fallback plus any deferred-unloaded / economy-hidden schemas
that never reach the wire). After rebasing onto Tool Source Economy
(PR #34), three economy tests still asserted the old providerTools.length
values; update them to the active counts, which now equal
modelToolNames().length in each case.

subject: feat(storage): pure-Node FileCredentialStore with cross-process lock + legacy migration (#32)
body:
A pure-Node credential store the headless runtime can use without Electron:
versioned credentials.json, owner-only 0600 atomic writes inside a 0700 dir,
and a cross-process write lock — an atomic mkdir of credentials.json.lock that
is never stolen (a held or leftover lock is waited on, then fails loud naming
the path), so two processes sharing one file can't lose a read-modify-write
update. The API is the generic getSecret/setSecret/deleteSecret over a
CredentialKind; the lock and the atomic writer stay module-internal and the
package exports only the typed store + the migration.

migrateLegacyCredentialFile imports a legacy (safeStorage-encrypted,
unversioned) credentials.json to v1 plaintext under the same lock, with the
crypto injected so a headless caller never runs it. It fails closed on an
unknown version, a malformed values map, a non-string value, or an unavailable
decryptor (an empty file still stamps to v1). chmod failures fail closed on
POSIX. At-rest posture is plaintext behind 0600 (file-first, #32 / SECURITY.md).

Part of #32 (PR A of two). Unblocks the headless lab #31.

subject: feat(desktop): move credentials.json off Electron safeStorage onto the shared store (#32)
body:
The desktop abandons safeStorage for credentials.json entirely — the live store
is now the pure-Node FileCredentialStore. credential-store.ts shrinks to a
one-time importer that injects a safeStorage-backed decryptor (base64-decode +
decryptString) into migrateLegacyCredentialFile, tombstoning the legacy file in
place and aborting intact if safeStorage is unavailable. safeStorage is injected
so the module no longer imports electron and is unit-testable. The importer
decrypts EVERY secret in the file (API keys, bot tokens, app secrets, proxy
password, gateway token, Tavily key) — any value left encrypted would become
permanently unreadable once the live store is pure-Node.

main.ts runs the migration in whenReady before any credential use, non-fatally;
onboarding reads through getSecret; dead setOAuthToken/getOAuthToken removed.

Part of #32 (PR A of two).

subject: refactor(headless): rename @maka/lab → @maka/headless
body:
The package is the single headless agent entry point, not just a
benchmark lab; eval is one mode of it. Pure rename — directory, package
name, bin (maka-lab → maka-headless), root workspaces, and internal
references. No behavior change; all 24 tests pass.

subject: feat(headless): harden the eval path and converge the CLI on the eval purpose
body:
Addresses an external review of the eval harness. The fix is an explicit
trust posture, never implicit host access:

- Fail closed: a model-backed backend (ai-sdk / pi-agent) is refused before a
  run starts — it would execute shell / network / file tools on the host, and
  the throwaway workspace is a copy, not a sandbox. Only the inert FakeBackend
  runs in-process. Real-model eval lands with the isolated executor (follow-up).
- Required grading boundary: TaskVerification.protectedPaths is no longer
  optional, so a config can't silently grade itself; the CLI rejects a spec
  whose task omits it.
- Honest exit codes: infrastructure failures (invalid spec, refused backend, a
  run that crashed before producing a result) exit non-zero; a run that
  completed and merely failed its verification stays exit 0 as valid data.
- Drop allowDangerousTools (it left shell_unsafe / network_send open) and deny
  every in-process permission request — nothing inert needs one.
- CLI `run` → `eval`: encode the purpose at the command boundary, so eval can
  never be handed host trust via a flag or spec field.
- Curate the public export surface; stop calling the throwaway workspace a
  "sandbox" in docs.

28 tests pass.

subject: fix(headless): review round 2 — unify failure signalling, validate in the engine, drop dead wiring
body:
- P2: a run whose backend reports failure without throwing now writes
  invocation.failure into ResultRecord.error, so the comparison table (⚠️) and
  the CLI exit code agree it was not a trustworthy run — no more ⚠️-but-exit-0
  that automation reads as success.
- P3: move the grading-boundary check into the engine. validateTaskVerification
  runs at the top of runExperiment, before any workspace / session / backend, so
  a direct (non-CLI) caller that omits protectedPaths fails fast; the CLI reuses
  the same function instead of a private duplicate.
- P3: remove the dead registerAiSdkBackend / LabConnection wiring and its public
  export. The real backend is refused this build anyway; its wiring returns with
  the isolated executor (follow-up PR). Public API is now the fake-eval surface.

30 tests pass.

subject: refactor(headless): shrink the public surface to the minimal fake-eval API
body:
Round-3 review (all non-blocking):

- registerBackends is now optional; runExperiment defaults to the inert
  FakeBackend, the only backend this build runs. Minimal usage is
  `runExperiment(config, task, { storageRoot })`; the CLI no longer passes a
  backend, and registerFakeBackend leaves the public index (backends.ts stays
  internal — the seam where real backends rejoin with the executor).
- Remove examples/fix-add (a real-model ai-sdk spec this build always refuses)
  and its README mention, so every shipped example actually runs. The
  real-backend example returns with the executor PR; refusal stays covered by
  the runner / CLI tests.

(mergeable=false was a transient post-push GitHub state; PR #42 reports
MERGEABLE / CLEAN, no rebase needed.)

31 tests pass.

subject: refactor(runtime): unify deferred loading and tool-source economy into ToolAvailabilityRuntime
body:
Collapse the two parallel tool-visibility mechanisms — PR #30 deferred tool
loading and PR #34 tool source economy — into one mechanism and one policy
(issue #37):

- One `economy` switch + configurable `coreToolNames`; grouping comes from
  `ToolAvailabilityConfig.groups`, not the per-tool `exposure` tag (removed
  from MakaTool). The now-dead `toolSource` tag is swept separately next.
- One `load_tools` connector, built by the runtime. Same-turn activation honors
  only `load_tools` (and only its `group` arg). The historical `load_tool`
  (PR #30) and `connect_tool_source` (PR #34) names are accepted solely when
  re-seeding prior-turn activations from the durable ledger — never advertised,
  never live in the current turn.
- One same-turn activation policy (prepareStep + execute-boundary gating);
  the next-request economy semantics are dropped.
- Delete tool-source-economy.ts, deferred-activation.ts, load-tool.ts and
  their tests; add tool-availability.ts + tool-availability.test.ts.
- Rename ToolSourceEconomyDiagnostic → ToolAvailabilityDiagnostic (record
  field toolSourceEconomy → toolAvailability) while keeping the historical
  *SourceIds shell vocabulary (a "source" id is a catalog group id).
- Wire main.ts through a single ToolAvailabilityConfig
  (economy = !MAKA_DISABLE_DEFERRED_TOOLS; groups = rive/office/browser); the
  runtime now produces the connector, so builtinTools no longer include it.

subject: refactor(runtime): drop the now-dead toolSource grouping tags
body:
With ToolSourceEconomyRuntime gone, nothing reads MakaTool.toolSource — the
unified runtime groups via ToolAvailabilityConfig.groups, not per-tool tags.
Remove the field (and its ToolSourceId import) plus the six builtin-tools tags
that set it. Pure dead-code removal; no behaviour change. The ToolSourceId
alias stays in @maka/core for the diagnostic's *SourceIds shell fields.

subject: refactor(ui): friendly presentation for the load_tools connector
body:
Recognize the unified `load_tools` connector (and the historical `load_tool`,
PR #30 — it shipped and returns the same `{ loaded: [...] }` shape) for the
localized "Load tools" card, reading the loaded group id from `group` with a
`namespace` fallback so replayed pre-unification sessions still render.

`connect_tool_source` (PR #34) is deliberately not presented: it never shipped,
so no such result exists, and its `{ tools: [...] }` shape differs from this
card's `{ loaded: [...] }`. The runtime still seeds from it for the durable
ledger (a separate concern that reads call args, not results).

subject: fix(storage): carry tool-availability diagnostic through TelemetryRepo.logs()
body:
logs() rebuilt each row field-by-field but dropped toolSchemaChangeReason and
toolAvailability, so the economy tool-surface diagnostic recorded on every LLM
call was never queryable. Pass both through (insert + on-disk normalize already
preserve them). Adds a precise telemetry-repo test round-tripping the diagnostic.

subject: lift Skills + Plan pages into reference-layout cards
body:
BIG VISIBLE redesign per WAWQAQ msg 5a461e0c — Skills page and 定时任务 (Plan/
Reminder) page now render as lifted white cards on the gray plate, matching
the upstream reference design's aux-panel signature instead of sitting flat.

Each page shell gets:
- 1px solid var(--border) outline
- 10px border-radius
- soft lift shadow: 0 1px 2px + 0 8px 24px -16px (foreground-tinted)
- generous inner padding (16/18px Skills, 18px Plan)
- 320ms cubic-bezier(.16,1,.3,1) entrance (opacity 0->1, translateY 6->0)

Surfaces touched:
- .maka-skill-library outer container — new card chrome + padding 16/18,
  gap 12 -> 14, page-shell entrance animation
- .maka-plan-shell outer container — new card chrome + padding 18,
  gap 10 -> 12, top padding tightened from clamp(16,3vh,32) to (16,3vh,24)
- new shared @keyframes maka-page-shell-enter
- prefers-reduced-motion + visual-smoke fixture both drop the animation

This is the structural pivot WAWQAQ asked for: pages no longer read as
flat lists on a flat canvas — they read as composed cards on a plate.
Inner sub-cards (.maka-skill-library-list, individual reminder cards) keep
their existing 1px borders, giving the reference layout's nested card hierarchy.

NOTE: icon test (dist/main/__tests__/localized-main-shell-contract.test.js:341)
is pre-existing red on origin/main after the icon PR #75 merged, unrelated
to this change. Verified by stashing this change and re-running — same red.

subject: fix(desktop): unify chrome icon size via --icon-size token (#81)
body:
The homepage chrome rendered icons at inconsistent sizes: left-nav icons
were hardcoded 18px (`.maka-nav-icon`) while the nav-primary glyph and every
`buttonVariants` icon sat at 1rem (15px via `size-4`). The nav was the lone
outlier.

Introduce one `--icon-size` token (16px) consumed by both surfaces:
`.maka-nav-icon` / `.maka-nav-primary-icon` / `.maka-nav-row` and the shared
`buttonVariants` (`[&_svg]:size-[var(--icon-size,1rem)]`, 1rem fallback keeps
@maka/ui self-contained). Nav and buttons now track one dial and can't drift
apart again.

Deliberately scoped NOT to flatten everything: dense-meta icons (12-14px:
status dots, footer actions, chips) and hero/emphasis icons (20px+) stay set
at their call sites — they are intentional hierarchy, not drift. A code
review of the earlier attempt flagged that a blanket `svg.lucide { width }`
rule would silently resize ~100 of those small glyphs (e.g. the Checkbox
check at 11px, SessionStatusIcon at 12px) and override their stroke; that
approach is dropped and the contract test now forbids it from returning.
`packages/ui/src/primitives/*` is a separate component library with its own
responsive icon scale and is untouched.

- maka-tokens.css: add --icon-size (16px)
- styles.css: nav icon width/height + nav-row grid column -> var(--icon-size)
- ui.tsx: buttonVariants svg size -> var(--icon-size,1rem)
- design-system.md §1.9 图标: register the token + the by-context tiers
- icon-system-contract.test.ts: assert nav + button consume the token, and
  forbid a blanket svg.lucide size rule; update the nav-icon assertion in
  localized-main-shell-contract.test.ts

Verified: 1536/1536 desktop tests green; renderer build compiles the token
(built CSS carries --icon-size:16px); screenshots confirm uniform 16px chrome
with dense status icons and hero glyphs unchanged.
